GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing
entries.body:
GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing

## Behaviour

`GreenMailExtension` extends `GreenMailProxy`. In `greenmail-junit5 2.1.3`, `GreenMailProxy.getGreenMail()` is declared `protected`:

```bash
$ javap -classpath greenmail-junit5-2.1.3.jar com.icegreen.greenmail.junit5.GreenMailExtension
...
protected com.icegreen.greenmail.util.GreenMail getGreenMail();
```

Attempting to call it from test code:

```java
GreenMail mail = GREEN_MAIL.getGreenMail(); // compile error: getGreenMail() has protected access
```

## Why This Matters

`GreenMail` (the bare class) has a `deliver(MimeMessage)` method and other useful internal APIs. The extension does not expose them. Since `getGreenMail()` is protected, you cannot retrieve the underlying instance to call them.

**Workaround:** In `@QuarkusTestResource` implementations that construct `GreenMail` directly (not via the extension), you hold the instance yourself as a static field — giving you full access to `deliver()`, `getManagers()`, etc.

## Why It's Undocumented

The Greenmail documentation and README make no mention of this access restriction. The Greenmail JUnit 5 guide shows the extension as a drop-in for the JUnit 4 Rule and implies equivalent functionality. The only way to discover the `protected` modifier is via `javap` on the jar.

**See also:** [GE-20260529-aa8445](GE-20260529-aa8445.md) — GreenMailExtension has no public deliver() method

Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods
entries.body:
Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods

## Place fireAsync() before internal dispatch to decouple delivery paths in @Transactional methods

### Symptom

A `@Transactional` method fires a CDI event and also calls an internal dispatch (channel write, database write, queue push). When the internal dispatch throws — rate-limit rejection, policy violation, missing resource — the CDI event silently never fires. The external delivery path is dead with no error.

### Context

Any method that must make two independent deliveries inside a `@Transactional` boundary:
one via CDI `fireAsync()` (external observers, notification systems) and one via an internal
service call. The pattern appears in watchdog alerting, domain event publishing, and audit
side-channels.

### Root cause

Ordering. If `fireAsync()` is placed *after* the internal dispatch and dispatch throws, the
stack unwinds before `fireAsync()` is reached. The two paths are not independent — external
delivery is silently contingent on internal dispatch succeeding.

### Fix

Place `fireAsync()` as the first statement inside the method, before the internal dispatch:

```java
private void fireAlert(Watchdog w, String summary, AlertContext context, Instant now) {
    // External delivery FIRST — not contingent on internal channel success.
    // A rate-limited, paused, or policy-rejected dispatch must not suppress this.
    alertEvents.fireAsync(new WatchdogAlertEvent(
            w.id, w.targetName, w.notificationChannel, summary, now, context));

// Internal dispatch SECOND — failure does not suppress the event already fired.
    Optional<Channel> ch = channelService.findByName(w.notificationChannel);
    if (ch.isEmpty()) {
        return;
    }
    messageService.dispatch(MessageDispatch.builder()
            .channelId(ch.get().id)
            .sender("system:watchdog")
            .type(MessageType.STATUS)
            .content(summary)
            .build());
}
```

The CDI event fires unconditionally (after the guard checks). The internal dispatch is the
conditional secondary path.

### Accepted trade-off

If the outer `@Transactional` rolls back after `fireAsync()` dispatches, the observer runs
for a state change that never committed — a ghost notification. The window is narrow in
practice (only a post-dispatch interceptor or infrastructure failure causes an outer rollback).
For alerting systems: accepted — false-positive alert is better than missed alert. For audit
or ledger systems: not acceptable — use a transactional outbox pattern instead.

### Why this is non-obvious

CDI events feel like side effects. By convention, developers put them at the *end* of a method,
after "the main work" is done. When the event IS the primary delivery path — not a side effect
— this ordering must invert. The distinction only surfaces when the two paths need to be
genuinely independent rather than sequential. Without this insight, a failing internal dispatch
silently kills external delivery with no error or log entry.

**See also:** [GE-20260512-0fe012](GE-20260512-0fe012.md) — fireAsync() dispatches immediately inside @Transactional (the timing gotcha this technique builds on)

entries.title: CaseHub.signal() is async (Vert.x event bus) — not a synchronous blackboard update
entries.body:
CaseHub.signal() is async (Vert.x event bus) — not a synchronous blackboard update

## CaseHub.signal() is async — fire-and-forget, not synchronous blackboard update

### Symptom

Code calls `caseHub.signal(caseId, "key", value)` expecting the value to be in `input.get("key")` when the next worker reads the blackboard. The key is missing or null.

### Context

`CaseHub.signal(UUID, String, Object)` returns `void`. Based on the signature and name, it appears to update the blackboard synchronously. In practice it uses Vert.x event bus `publish()` — fire-and-forget — and returns before the blackboard is updated.

Occurs whenever you call `signal()` and then rely on a worker reading that key in the same or next Quartz execution window.

### Root Cause

`CaseHub.signal()` delegates to `CaseHubRuntimeImpl.signal()`, which delegates to `CaseHubReactor.signal()`:

```java
// CaseHubReactor.signal()
void signal(UUID caseId, String path, Object value) {
    eventBus.publish(SIGNAL_RECEIVED, new SignalReceivedEvent(caseId, path, value));
}
```

`eventBus.publish()` is fire-and-forget on the Vert.x event bus. It returns before any subscriber processes the event. The blackboard update happens asynchronously on the reactor pool.

Compare with `startCase()`, which uses `eventBus.request()` (request-reply) to block until the case is initialised.

### Fix

For getting the case ID inside a worker lambda, use `WorkerExecutionContext.current().caseId()` instead of signalling. The engine populates `WorkerExecutionContext` on the Quartz thread immediately before invoking the worker function — it is synchronous and guaranteed to be present.

```java
// In a worker lambda:
final UUID caseId = WorkerExecutionContext.current().caseId();
// caseId is the CaseInstance UUID — always non-null
```

`WorkerExecutionContext` is in `io.casehub.engine.common.spi.context`. Its `caseId()` field comes from the `WorkerContext` record populated by `QuartzWorkerExecutionJob` before any worker executes.

**✅ Resolved (engine#559, 2026-06-23)** — `DefaultWorkerExecutor.executeFlow()` now calls `WorkerExecutionContext.set(context)` before delegating to `FlowWorkerExecutor`, matching the behaviour of `executeSync()`. `WorkerExecutionContext.current().caseId()` is safe inside FuncDSL lambdas. See casehubio/aml#66 for the AML migration that removed the Sync workaround.

### Why Non-Obvious

`signal()` returns void, has no async suffix, and the `CaseHub` class Javadoc does not explain the delivery model. The contrast with `startCase()` (which blocks) is not documented. A developer would reasonably assume void = synchronous.

entries.title: Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422
entries.body:
Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422

## Symptom

Code compiles with a `Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` reference in a `WebApplicationException` or `Response.status()` call, but the IDE shows an error and the build fails:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
  location: class Response.Status
```

## Root cause

`Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` (HTTP 422) was added in JAX-RS 3.1 / Jakarta EE 10. Projects targeting Jakarta EE 9 / JAX-RS 3.0 (which Quarkus 3.x may use depending on the BOM) don't have this constant.

## Fix

Use the raw integer `422` directly:

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()
entries.body:
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()

## What Happened

Code used `Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` to offload blocking work onto a worker pool in a Mutiny pipeline. The method name is logical (Mutiny has `runSubscriptionOn` for blocking work) but the method does not exist. The compile error is clear but the fix is non-obvious because no similar `getDefaultBlockingExecutor` pattern appears in Mutiny documentation.

## Why This Is Non-obvious

The Mutiny `Infrastructure` class has `getDefaultExecutor()` — the default worker pool suitable for blocking work when combined with `runSubscriptionOn`. There is no separately named "blocking" executor. The distinction between the IO thread and the worker pool is handled by `runSubscriptionOn` itself, not by a separate executor variant. The name "blocking" is a common mental model but doesn't map to any Mutiny API.

## Fix

```java
// Wrong — does not exist:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor())

// Correct:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultExecutor())
```

## Invalidation Triggers

Revisit if Mutiny adds a named `getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` alias in a future release.

**See also:** [GE-20260602-c38360](GE-20260602-c38360.md) — PanacheQuery.stream() does not exist in Quarkus Hibernate Reactive Panache

**See also:** [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist

**See also:** [GE-20260621-cb4492](GE-20260621-cb4492.md) — drools-engine artifact does not exist at Drools 10.1.0 | [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist (same class of API-that-doesn't-exist gotcha)

OpenClaw pluggable context engine — kind:context-engine delegates full context assembly to a plugin
entries.body:
OpenClaw pluggable context engine — kind:context-engine delegates full context assembly to a plugin

## What it is

If an OpenClaw plugin declares `kind: "context-engine"` in its plugin metadata and is
selected via `plugins.slots.contextEngine` in config, OpenClaw hands off ALL context
assembly to that plugin — not just the `before_prompt_build` hook.

This includes: context construction, conversation history management, `/compact`
lifecycle hooks, and subagent context. The plugin controls the full context window.

## Context

OpenClaw's default context pipeline processes bootstrap files, session history, and tool
data to build each agent turn's context window. The `before_prompt_build` hook lets you
inject content into this pipeline at one point. The context engine replaces the entire
pipeline.

## How to activate

```json
// openclaw config
{
  "plugins": {
    "slots": {
      "contextEngine": "my-context-engine-plugin"
    }
  }
}
```

The plugin must expose a `kind: "context-engine"` declaration and implement the context
lifecycle hooks OpenClaw calls into: context build, compaction, subagent context prep.

## Why this matters

For a CaseHub × OpenClaw integration (`casehub-openclaw`), the context engine is the
right integration point if you need to inject Qhorus channel history, CaseMemoryStore
facts, and WorkerContextProvider lineage as a unified context package. More powerful than
`before_prompt_build` alone, which only injects at one point in the pipeline.

## Why this is non-obvious

The official docs describe `before_prompt_build`, `before_model_resolve`, and other
individual hooks. The pluggable context engine is mentioned briefly in config documentation
under `plugins.slots.contextEngine` but not in any of the hook or integration guides.
You would only find it by reading the plugin system config reference or the source.

entries.title: GraalVM tracing agent as sole discovery path for ONNX Runtime + DJL native-image config
entries.body:
GraalVM tracing agent as sole discovery path for ONNX Runtime + DJL native-image config

### The technique
When building GraalVM native images with JNI libraries that have no upstream reachability metadata, the tracing agent in JVM mode is the only viable discovery path for the complete config. No manual inspection of source code or documentation can reliably produce the full set of JNI, reflection, and resource entries.

### How to apply

Run the application in JVM mode with the tracing agent:

```bash
java -agentlib:native-image-agent=config-output-dir=target/agent-config \
  -jar target/quarkus-app/quarkus-run.jar \
  <application-args>
```

Exercise all code paths that use JNI. The agent generates `reachability-metadata.json` capturing every reflection, JNI, and resource access. Copy the output to `src/main/resources/META-INF/native-image/<group>/<artifact>/`.

### Why this is non-obvious
The intuitive approach is to inspect library source for `native` method declarations, `System.loadLibrary()` calls, and reflection usage — then manually register each one. This fails because:

1. **Rust→Java JNI callbacks are invisible** — DJL's tokenizer has Rust code that calls `JNIEnv::NewObject` to create Java objects. These callbacks don't appear in Java source at all.
2. **JNA bridge classes are implicit** — DJL uses JNA, which has its own JNI surface (`com.sun.jna.*`) that must be registered. Dozens of JNA classes need JNI access.
3. **Transitive resource dependencies** — ONNX Runtime bundles multiple `.dylib` files (core, JNI bridge, providers, WebGPU) that all need resource registration.
4. **No upstream metadata exists** — as of June 2026, ONNX Runtime, DJL, LangChain4j, quarkiverse/quarkus-langchain4j, and the GraalVM reachability metadata repo all lack native-image config for these libraries.

### What developers would normally do instead
Manually inspect source, write JNI/reflection config by hand, and iterate on build failures. This works for simple libraries but misses Rust→Java callbacks and implicit JNA requirements, leading to hours of debugging opaque runtime failures (Rust panics, UnsatisfiedLinkError) with no clear diagnostic.

**See also:** [GE-20260606-025601](GE-20260606-025601.md) — ONNX Runtime --initialize-at-run-time must be package-level (the specific gotcha this technique addresses) | [GE-20260606-aab62a](GE-20260606-aab62a.md) — DJL 0.36.0 ships partial native-image metadata | [GE-20260606-7bf5dd](GE-20260606-7bf5dd.md) — DJL CharSpan JNI callback causes Rust panic in GraalVM native image

entries.title: Quarkus getCredentialTransport() declaring Bearer transport conflicts with OIDC mechanism — breaks @TestSecurity and causes mechanism ambiguity
entries.body:
Quarkus getCredentialTransport() declaring Bearer transport conflicts with OIDC mechanism — breaks @TestSecurity and causes mechanism ambiguity

### Symptom

After adding a custom `HttpAuthenticationMechanism` with `getCredentialTransport()` returning `HttpCredentialTransport(Type.AUTHORIZATION, "bearer", "plugin-token")`, all `@TestSecurity` tests return 500 (OIDC server connection refused) — including tests for endpoints not handled by the custom mechanism.

### Context

Application has `quarkus-oidc` on the classpath (for JWT validation on some endpoints) and a custom mechanism for static bearer token validation on other endpoints. Both declare `Type.AUTHORIZATION` with `"bearer"` as the transport type.

### What was tried (didn't work)

1. Using the 3-arg constructor `HttpCredentialTransport(Type.AUTHORIZATION, "bearer", "plugin-token")` — `typeTarget="bearer"` matches OIDC's transport type, causing ambiguity
2. Combining `auth-mechanism=plugin-token` config with the credential transport — breaks @TestSecurity (see GE-20260628-04a38c)

### Root cause

Quarkus uses `getCredentialTransport()` for mechanism deconfliction. When two mechanisms declare the same `typeTarget` (`"bearer"`), Quarkus cannot distinguish between them for challenge sending and mechanism selection. The OIDC mechanism's challenge flow then fires for requests it shouldn't handle, triggering OIDC discovery against an absent server.

The Javadoc explicitly warns: "Note that using multiple HTTP authentication mechanisms to use the same credential transport type can lead to unexpected authentication failures."

### Fix

Return `Uni.createFrom().nullItem()` from `getCredentialTransport()`:

```java
@Override
public Uni<HttpCredentialTransport> getCredentialTransport(RoutingContext context) {
    return Uni.createFrom().nullItem();
}
```

Per Javadoc: null means "this mechanism cannot interfere with other mechanisms." The mechanism still works — Quarkus calls `authenticate()` via proactive auth. The path guard in `authenticate()` provides isolation.

### Why this is non-obvious

The 3-arg constructor's Javadoc suggests `authenticationScheme` can be different from `typeTarget` for routing purposes. The expectation is that a distinct scheme ("plugin-token" vs "bearer") would prevent transport-type conflicts. In practice, the `typeTarget` comparison is what causes ambiguity, not the scheme.

entries.title: quarkus-oidc-client on the classpath triggers Keycloak DevServices even when tests use static token auth
entries.body:
quarkus-oidc-client on the classpath triggers Keycloak DevServices even when tests use static token auth

## Symptom

Tests using `quarkus-oidc-client` take 60–90 seconds to start (or OOMKill) because
a full Keycloak container is being launched — even though every test path uses a
static bearer token and the OIDC client is never called.

## Context

A module exposes two auth paths: a static bearer token (`casehub.platform.scim.token`)
and an OIDC client credentials flow (`quarkus.oidc-client.scim.*`). Tests configure
the static token only. Keycloak DevServices fires anyway because Quarkus sees
`quarkus-oidc-client` on the classpath and starts DevServices unconditionally —
it does not check whether any code path will actually acquire a token at runtime.

## Root Cause

Quarkus DevServices extension activation is classpath-driven, not call-path-driven.
The presence of `quarkus-oidc-client` is sufficient to start Keycloak DevServices
regardless of whether `quarkus.oidc-client.*.auth-server-url` is configured or any
OIDC token acquisition will occur.

## Fix

Disable Keycloak DevServices explicitly in the test `application.properties`:

```properties
# Tests use static token — OIDC client is never called; Keycloak DevServices
# would start a heavy container for nothing.
quarkus.keycloak.devservices.enabled=false
```

**Required condition:** the OIDC client bean must be injected as `Instance<OidcClient>`
(lazy CDI resolution) rather than `OidcClient` directly. With `Instance<>`, Quarkus
starts successfully even though `quarkus.oidc-client.*.auth-server-url` is absent —
the bean is only resolved if the code actually calls `.get()`. Direct injection fails
at startup with a deployment error when the OIDC client is not configured.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The connection between extension presence and DevServices activation is not documented
at the `quarkus-oidc-client` level. The symptom (slow/failing container startup) points
at Keycloak internals rather than at the decision to start Keycloak at all. The fix
requires knowing both that DevServices is classpath-triggered AND that `Instance<>` is
needed to survive startup without an `auth-server-url`.

entries.title: SPLADE cocondenser-ensembledistil is CC NonCommercial — permissive alternative at equivalent quality
entries.body:
SPLADE cocondenser-ensembledistil is CC NonCommercial — permissive alternative at equivalent quality

### Symptom
Evaluating SPLADE models for a hybrid RAG pipeline. Most tutorials and benchmarks reference `naver/splade-cocondenser-ensembledistil` as the standard SPLADE model.

### Context
Selecting a SPLADE model for sparse embeddings in a commercial platform (casehub-neural-text). The naver model is the most-cited in blog posts, Pinecone docs, and academic papers.

### Root cause
`naver/splade-cocondenser-ensembledistil` is released under Creative Commons NonCommercial. The license is not prominent on the HuggingFace model card — you discover it by checking the repo's license field, not from the README or usage examples.

### Fix
Use `prithivida/Splade_PP_en_v1` instead. It achieves equivalent MRR@10 (~37.22 vs ~37.2-38.3 for the naver model) with lower FLOPS and ~10% fewer tokens. It initialises from vanilla `bert-base-uncased` rather than CoCondenser pre-trained weights, and is released under a more permissive license.

ONNX export available at `onnx-models/Splade_PP_en_v1-onnx` on HuggingFace (~436MB).

### Why this is non-obvious
The "official" model from the paper authors is the natural first choice. Tutorials don't mention the license. The permissive alternative (`Splade_PP_en_v1`) is an independent reimplementation — not obvious that it achieves equivalent quality without checking benchmarks directly.

entries.title: Quarkus WebAuthn Actual HTTP Endpoint Paths (Only Discoverable via Bytecode)
entries.body:
Quarkus WebAuthn Actual HTTP Endpoint Paths (Only Discoverable via Bytecode)

## Quarkus WebAuthn Actual HTTP Endpoint Paths (Only Discoverable via Bytecode)

**ID:** GE-0078
**Stack:** Quarkus 3.9.x (`quarkus-security-webauthn`), Vert.x 4.5.x

**What exists:** The Quarkus WebAuthn extension registers four HTTP endpoints under the non-application root (`/q/`):

| Endpoint | Method | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `/q/webauthn/register` | POST | Step 1: server returns WebAuthn challenge options |
| `/q/webauthn/login` | POST | Step 1: server returns WebAuthn login challenge |
| `/q/webauthn/callback` | POST | Step 2: client submits credential/assertion |
| `/q/webauthn/webauthn.js` | GET | Quarkus-provided JS helper library |

**Why it's undocumented:** The Quarkus WebAuthn guide does not list these paths explicitly. The paths appear only as string constants in `WebAuthnRecorder.class` — discoverable via:
```bash
javap -verbose WebAuthnRecorder.class | grep "webauthn/"
```

**Common mistake:** Most WebAuthn tutorials describe a two-step REST pattern with `/options` and `/finish` endpoints. Quarkus uses a single-step per phase: there are no `/options` or `/finish` endpoints.

**Verification:**
```bash
curl http://localhost:7777/q/webauthn/webauthn.js           # 200 (JS helper)
curl -X POST http://localhost:7777/q/webauthn/register \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{}'               # 400 (route exists)
curl -X POST http://localhost:7777/q/webauthn/register/options # 404 (doesn't exist)
```

*Score: 13/15 · Included because: genuinely absent from official docs, only findable via bytecode inspection, common wrong assumption causes hours of debugging · Reservation: Quarkus-version-specific*

entries.title: Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422
entries.body:
Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422

## Symptom

Code compiles with a `Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` reference in a `WebApplicationException` or `Response.status()` call, but the IDE shows an error and the build fails:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
  location: class Response.Status
```

## Root cause

`Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` (HTTP 422) was added in JAX-RS 3.1 / Jakarta EE 10. Projects targeting Jakarta EE 9 / JAX-RS 3.0 (which Quarkus 3.x may use depending on the BOM) don't have this constant.

## Fix

Use the raw integer `422` directly:

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

entries.title: TIMESTAMPTZ not recognised by H2 in MODE=PostgreSQL — use TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE
entries.body:
TIMESTAMPTZ not recognised by H2 in MODE=PostgreSQL — use TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE

## Context

Writing Flyway migrations for a Quarkus module that uses PostgreSQL in production and H2
with `MODE=PostgreSQL` in `@QuarkusTest`. The migration uses `TIMESTAMPTZ` (PostgreSQL's
abbreviated type name for `TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE`).

## Symptom

Flyway migration fails during `@QuarkusTest` startup with a SQL syntax error even though
`MODE=PostgreSQL` is configured. Production (PostgreSQL) works fine.

## Root Cause

`TIMESTAMPTZ` is a PostgreSQL-specific abbreviation. H2 implements only a subset of
PostgreSQL syntax in `MODE=PostgreSQL` — it recognises the full form
`TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE` but not the abbreviated `TIMESTAMPTZ`.

## Fix

Use the full form in Flyway migrations:

```sql
-- Fails in H2 even with MODE=PostgreSQL
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,

-- Works in both PostgreSQL and H2 MODE=PostgreSQL
created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE NOT NULL,
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

PostgreSQL documentation and tooling widely use `TIMESTAMPTZ` — it appears in pg_dump
output, official PostgreSQL tutorials, and StackOverflow answers. `MODE=PostgreSQL` in H2
creates an expectation that PostgreSQL-idiomatic SQL will work. The error message points
to a SQL syntax failure, not to a type-name unsupported issue, making diagnosis indirect.

**See also:** [GE-20260603-86f2a9](GE-20260603-86f2a9.md) — H2 does not support INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING | [GE-20260605-0ffc19](GE-20260605-0ffc19.md) — H2 does not support SIMILAR TO in CHECK constraints

**See also:** [GE-20260617-03c1a3](GE-20260617-03c1a3.md) — H2 MODE=PostgreSQL ON CONFLICT syntax limitations

entries.title: ChatModel.doChat(ChatRequest) is the override point for test doubles — not chat(ChatRequest)
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()
entries.body:
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()

## What Happened

Code used `Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` to offload blocking work onto a worker pool in a Mutiny pipeline. The method name is logical (Mutiny has `runSubscriptionOn` for blocking work) but the method does not exist. The compile error is clear but the fix is non-obvious because no similar `getDefaultBlockingExecutor` pattern appears in Mutiny documentation.

## Why This Is Non-obvious

The Mutiny `Infrastructure` class has `getDefaultExecutor()` — the default worker pool suitable for blocking work when combined with `runSubscriptionOn`. There is no separately named "blocking" executor. The distinction between the IO thread and the worker pool is handled by `runSubscriptionOn` itself, not by a separate executor variant. The name "blocking" is a common mental model but doesn't map to any Mutiny API.

## Fix

```java
// Wrong — does not exist:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor())

// Correct:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultExecutor())
```

## Invalidation Triggers

Revisit if Mutiny adds a named `getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` alias in a future release.

**See also:** [GE-20260602-c38360](GE-20260602-c38360.md) — PanacheQuery.stream() does not exist in Quarkus Hibernate Reactive Panache

**See also:** [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist

**See also:** [GE-20260621-cb4492](GE-20260621-cb4492.md) — drools-engine artifact does not exist at Drools 10.1.0 | [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist (same class of API-that-doesn't-exist gotcha)

entries.title: XML comment containing '--' crashes Maven with Non-parseable POM
entries.body:
XML comment containing '--' crashes Maven with Non-parseable POM

## Symptom

Maven fails to parse `pom.xml` with an opaque error during a normal build:

```
[FATAL] Non-parseable POM /path/to/pom.xml: in comment after two dashes (--)
next character must be > not a (position: END_TAG seen ...some text --a... @34:15)
```

The build was working before. No XML structure was changed — only a comment was added.

## Context

Adding descriptive text inside an XML comment (`<!-- ... -->`) that happens to
contain two consecutive hyphens (`--`). Common when copy-pasting error messages,
command-line flags, or markdown content into comments.

Example triggering content:
```xml
<!-- KNOWN ISSUE: bootstrap fails with "Unsupported value type: [ALL-UNNAMED]"
     --add-opens args cause Json.appendValue() to fail -->
```

## Root Cause

The XML specification forbids `--` anywhere inside an XML comment. The string `-->`
ends a comment, but `--` alone (not followed by `>`) is also illegal and causes a
parse error. Maven's XML parser correctly rejects it; the error message is
misleading because it points to the character *after* the `--`, not the `--` itself.

## Fix

Replace `--` with an alternative that preserves readability:

```xml
<!-- KNOWN ISSUE: bootstrap fails with "Unsupported value type: [ALL-UNNAMED]".
     The add-opens args in the Jlama extension cause Json.appendValue() to fail. -->
```

Or use a Unicode dash (—) if the `--` is cosmetic, or break the comment to
avoid the double-hyphen entirely.

## Why Non-Obvious

XML comments look like HTML comments where `--` is harmless. Most developers
assume the only special sequence inside a comment is `-->`. The error message
reports the character *after* the `--`, making it appear the problem is
elsewhere in the file. The actual offending `--` may be many lines away from
the reported position.

entries.title: U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it
entries.body:
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it

### What happened
A field validator banning BiDi control characters to prevent prompt injection implemented a standard check: reject codepoints 0–31 (C0), 127–159 (C1), U+200E/200F (LRM/RLM), U+202A–202E (LRE/RLE/PDF/LRO/RLO), and U+2066–2069 (LRI/RLI/FSI/PDI). Code review caught that U+061C was missing. The validator would have passed strings containing the Arabic Letter Mark.

### Root cause
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark, ALM) was added in Unicode 6.3 as a BiDi control character. It is in the Arabic block (U+0600–U+06FF), not in any standard control character range (C0: 0x00–0x1F, C1: 0x80–0x9F). It has no visible glyph and controls BiDi text direction.

Standard documentation and implementations of "ban control characters" focus on C0/C1 ranges. The dedicated BiDi control ranges (LRM/RLM/LRE etc.) are more widely known. U+061C predates none of these — it was simply added later and sits in an unexpected block.

### Fix
Add explicit check for U+061C alongside other BiDi controls:

```java
private static boolean isBanned(final int cp) {
    if (cp <= 0x001F) return true;                      // C0 control chars
    if (cp >= 0x007F && cp <= 0x009F) return true;     // DEL + C1 control chars
    if (cp == 0x061C) return true;                      // ALM — Arabic Letter Mark (BiDi, Unicode 6.3)
    if (cp == 0x200E || cp == 0x200F) return true;     // LRM, RLM
    if (cp >= 0x202A && cp <= 0x202E) return true;     // LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO
    if (cp >= 0x2066 && cp <= 0x2069) return true;     // LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI
    if (cp == 0x200B || cp == 0xFEFF) return true;     // ZWSP, BOM/ZWNBSP
    if (cp == 0x200C || cp == 0x200D) return true;     // ZWNJ, ZWJ
    if (cp == 0x2028 || cp == 0x2029) return true;     // LINE SEP, PARA SEP
    return false;
}
```

### Why this is non-obvious
The Unicode BiDi control characters everyone knows (LRM, RLM, LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO, LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI) are all in the General Punctuation block (U+2000–U+206F). U+061C is in the Arabic block. No standard "control character" resource bundles it with the others. The Unicode BiDi Algorithm spec mentions it, but security-focused implementations typically enumerate the well-known ranges without reading the full BiDi spec for outliers.

entries.title: static new ObjectMapper() silently fails to serialize Instant in Quarkus — use @Inject ObjectMapper
entries.body:
static new ObjectMapper() silently fails to serialize Instant in Quarkus — use @Inject ObjectMapper

## Symptom

An SSE endpoint (or any endpoint) that serialises `SessionResponse` objects silently returns `[]` (empty array) instead of the actual workers. No exception is visible to the caller — the exception is caught internally and the fallback value is returned. The server logs may show a `com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidDefinitionException` at DEBUG level.

## Context

`SessionResource` in Claudony had:
```java
private static final ObjectMapper MAPPER = new ObjectMapper();
```

`SessionResponse` contains `Instant createdAt` and `Instant lastActive`. Serializing a `List<SessionResponse>` through this `MAPPER` threw:
```
com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidDefinitionException:
Java 8 date/time type `java.time.Instant` not supported by default:
add Module "com.fasterxml.jackson.datatype:jackson-datatype-jsr310"
```

The exception was caught in the `buildCaseSnapshot` helper and the fallback `"data: []"` was returned silently.

## Root Cause

`new ObjectMapper()` creates a bare instance with no modules registered. Quarkus configures its CDI-managed `ObjectMapper` bean with `JavaTimeModule` (and other modules like `Jdk8Module`) automatically. A static field bypasses this configuration.

## Fix

Replace the static field with CDI injection:

```java
// WRONG — no JavaTimeModule
private static final ObjectMapper MAPPER = new ObjectMapper();

// CORRECT — uses Quarkus-configured instance with all modules
@Inject
ObjectMapper MAPPER;
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The class had a `static final` `ObjectMapper` that worked fine for other uses (simple POJOs without time fields). The failure only surfaced when the snapshot function started serialising `SessionResponse` with `Instant` fields. Because the exception was caught with a fallback, the endpoint returned valid JSON (`[]`) — not an error — making it very hard to notice.

entries.title: GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing
entries.body:
GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing

## Behaviour

`GreenMailExtension` extends `GreenMailProxy`. In `greenmail-junit5 2.1.3`, `GreenMailProxy.getGreenMail()` is declared `protected`:

```bash
$ javap -classpath greenmail-junit5-2.1.3.jar com.icegreen.greenmail.junit5.GreenMailExtension
...
protected com.icegreen.greenmail.util.GreenMail getGreenMail();
```

Attempting to call it from test code:

```java
GreenMail mail = GREEN_MAIL.getGreenMail(); // compile error: getGreenMail() has protected access
```

## Why This Matters

`GreenMail` (the bare class) has a `deliver(MimeMessage)` method and other useful internal APIs. The extension does not expose them. Since `getGreenMail()` is protected, you cannot retrieve the underlying instance to call them.

**Workaround:** In `@QuarkusTestResource` implementations that construct `GreenMail` directly (not via the extension), you hold the instance yourself as a static field — giving you full access to `deliver()`, `getManagers()`, etc.

## Why It's Undocumented

The Greenmail documentation and README make no mention of this access restriction. The Greenmail JUnit 5 guide shows the extension as a drop-in for the JUnit 4 Rule and implies equivalent functionality. The only way to discover the `protected` modifier is via `javap` on the jar.

**See also:** [GE-20260529-aa8445](GE-20260529-aa8445.md) — GreenMailExtension has no public deliver() method

'Reactive' Quarkus service classes may use blocking services or Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative in-memory stores don't intercept those paths
entries.body:
'Reactive' Quarkus service classes may use blocking services or Panache statics internally — CDI @Alternative in-memory stores don't intercept those paths

### What is undocumented

A service class annotated `@Alternative` and named with "Reactive" may internally use a `blockingService` field (injected blocking CDI service) for write operations and `Entity.find()` (a Panache static) for read operations. CDI `@Alternative @Priority(1)` in-memory stores only intercept at CDI injection points — Panache statics go directly to the underlying database regardless of what alternatives are selected.

### Context

In Quarkus Qhorus (casehubio/qhorus), `ReactiveQhorusMcpTools`:
- `sendMessage()` delegates internally to `blockingMessageService` → `MessageService.send()` → `messageStore.put()` (uses the `MessageStore` CDI alternative, i.e. `InMemoryMessageStore` in tests)
- `getChannelTimeline()` delegates to `blockingGetChannelTimeline()` → `Message.<Message>find(...)` (Panache static — queries H2 directly, bypasses `MessageStore` entirely)

Result: messages written by `sendMessage()` land in `InMemoryMessageStore` (in-memory). Messages read by `getChannelTimeline()` come from H2 (empty in tests). The two paths never share data.

### Discovery

Only visible by reading the service implementation source. The class name and method signatures suggest a uniform reactive abstraction. Not mentioned in any documentation.

### Impact on tests

Tests that call `sendMessage()` then verify the timeline via `getChannelTimeline()` always see an empty timeline in test setups using `@Alternative` in-memory stores, even though the message was "successfully" posted.

### Fix / Workaround

Identify the actual data path for each operation you need to test:
1. Check which CDI service field the MCP tool method delegates to
2. Check whether that service uses CDI store injection or Panache statics
3. For end-to-end write→read tests: use the real H2 database (don't rely on in-memory stores for both sides)

Alternatively, file an upstream issue to make the read path use the same abstraction as the write path.

**See also:** [GE-20260512-66d997](GE-20260512-66d997.md) — Panache static methods bypass CDI @Alternative stores

casehub-engine persistence-memory Maven profile required for all engine tests without Docker
entries.body:
casehub-engine persistence-memory Maven profile required for all engine tests without Docker

## What It Is

casehub-engine has a `persistence-memory` Maven profile in `engine/pom.xml` that activates the in-memory persistence layer for tests. Without this profile, engine integration tests attempt to use Testcontainers (Docker) and fail with class-loader errors when Docker is not available.

## How to Use It

```bash
# Engine tests without Docker (correct command):
TESTCONTAINERS_RYUK_DISABLED=true mvn test -pl engine \
  -Ppersistence-memory \
  -Dexcludes="**/SignalTest.java"
```

The profile sets `quarkus.test.profile=memory` via `engine.test.extra.jvm.args` and adds `casehub-persistence-memory` as a test dependency.

## Why Not Documented

The profile was added in PR #86 ("test(engine): add Maven profiles for dual-persistence testing") with no update to CLAUDE.md or README. The profile name and its requirement for Docker-free development was only discoverable by reading `engine/pom.xml`.

## Notes

- Without `-Ppersistence-memory`, tests `SignalDedupExtendedTest` and `WorkerScheduleDedupTest` fail at JUnit class discovery (not at test execution) when Docker is unavailable
- `SignalTest` has a separate pre-existing class-loader issue unrelated to Docker — always exclude it
- CLAUDE.md has since been updated to document this requirement

**See also:** GE-20260420-4a62d3 (casehub-persistence-memory as test dep fails CDI discovery — copy sources instead)

entries.title: In-memory test doubles are not rolled back when @Transactional rolls back — JTA and non-JTA writes diverge
entries.body:
In-memory test doubles are not rolled back when @Transactional rolls back — JTA and non-JTA writes diverge

## Symptom

A `@Transactional` method writes to both a JPA store and an in-memory `@Alternative` store. A downstream call in the same transaction throws an exception. After rollback:

- The JPA store is clean (the row was rolled back) ✅
- The in-memory store still shows the write ❌

Tests that assert on the in-memory store state after a simulated failure see stale data and pass or fail incorrectly.

## Context

`@Alternative @ApplicationScoped` in-memory stores (e.g. `MemoryPlanItemStore`) are used in `@QuarkusTest` to avoid requiring a real database. A `@Transactional` handler calls the in-memory store's `updateStatus()` before creating a JPA entity. The creation fails (simulated by a `FailingWorkItemStore`). The JTA transaction rolls back the JPA work, but the in-memory map write was already done and has no JTA enlistment — it stays.

## Root cause

JTA rollback only undoes work that enlisted in the transaction via `XAResource` or a `Synchronization`. A plain `ConcurrentHashMap` write does not enlist. There is no undo mechanism.

## Fix

**Design fix (preferred):** Reorder operations so the non-JTA store write happens *after* the JTA-participating work, and *only* if no exception occurred:

```java
// JPA work first (participates in @Transactional)
workItemService.create(request);
// In-memory write after — only reached if JPA work succeeded
planItemStore.save(caseId, planItemId, bindingName, RUNNING, createdAt);
item.markRunning();
```

If JPA work throws, the method exits before the in-memory write. If the in-memory write is inside the transaction but before JPA work and JPA work throws, the in-memory write is NOT rolled back.

**Test-only fix:** Call `store.clear()` in `@BeforeEach` to reset state between tests regardless of what happened in the previous test.

## Why this is non-obvious

`@Transactional` feels like a safety net — everything in the method gets rolled back. In reality, only JTA participants are rolled back. In-memory stores that substitute for JPA stores in tests are specifically designed to *not* require JTA, which means they also don't participate in rollback. The discrepancy only surfaces when testing failure paths.

renamePsiElementProcessor Requires order="first" for getPostRenameCallback to Fire
entries.body:
renamePsiElementProcessor Requires order="first" for getPostRenameCallback to Fire

## renamePsiElementProcessor Requires order="first" for getPostRenameCallback to Fire

**ID:** GE-0080
**Stack:** IntelliJ Platform (any version), `plugin.xml` extension registration
**Symptom:** A custom `RenamePsiElementProcessor` is invoked — `prepareRenaming()` runs and `canProcessElement()` returns true — but `getPostRenameCallback()` never fires. The rename completes normally; no error is thrown. Custom post-rename logic is silently skipped.
**Context:** Any plugin registering a `RenamePsiElementProcessor` that relies on `getPostRenameCallback()`.

### Root cause

IntelliJ calls `getPostRenameCallback()` only on the "primary" processor — the one returned by `RenamePsiElementProcessor.forElement(element)`. This method iterates registered processors in registration order and returns the first whose `canProcessElement()` returns true. The bundled Java plugin registers a `JavaRenameRefactoringSupport` processor at default priority. Without explicit ordering, it wins the election and its `getPostRenameCallback()` (which returns null for most elements) runs instead of the custom processor's.

### Fix

Register with `order="first"` in `plugin.xml`:
```xml
<renamePsiElementProcessor
    implementation="com.example.MyRenameProcessor"
    order="first"/>
```

### Why non-obvious

`prepareRenaming()` is called on ALL processors whose `canProcessElement()` returns true — it appears the processor is working. Only `getPostRenameCallback()` (and `renameElement()` for the primary processor) is restricted to the primary. The distinction between "all processors" and "primary processor" is not documented in the `RenamePsiElementProcessor` Javadoc. The `order` attribute in `plugin.xml` is documented for some extension points but not called out as required here.

**See also:** GE-0079 (spurious space — same rename processor pipeline), GE-0081 (PsiElement invalidation — same rename lifecycle)

*Score: 14/15 · Included because: completely silent failure, 100% reproducible, anyone using getPostRenameCallback() will hit this · Reservation: none*

entries.title: OpenClaw WhatsApp uses Baileys (personal tier) not Meta Cloud API (business tier)
entries.body:
OpenClaw WhatsApp uses Baileys (personal tier) not Meta Cloud API (business tier)

## Context
When integrating a system with OpenClaw's WhatsApp delivery and comparing it
against a direct Meta Cloud API implementation, an apparent overlap exists.
They appear to solve the same problem — sending WhatsApp messages.

## What's actually happening
OpenClaw's WhatsApp channel uses **Baileys** — a library that reverse-engineers
the WhatsApp Web WebSocket protocol. This is personal-account tier, not the
official WhatsApp Business API.

| | OpenClaw (Baileys) | Direct (Meta Cloud API) |
|---|---|---|
| Account type | Personal WhatsApp | WhatsApp Business |
| Auth | WhatsApp account credentials | API token + Phone Number ID |
| Direction | Bidirectional (receive + send) | Outbound only |
| Status | Unofficial / reverse-engineered | Official / stable |
| Cost | Free | Per-message after threshold |

## Why this matters architecturally
The two implementations are not interchangeable and serve different use cases.
Baileys reaches personal numbers; Meta Cloud API requires a Business account.
There is no credential deduplication opportunity between a system using the
official API and OpenClaw — they operate at different tiers entirely.

## Why this is non-obvious
OpenClaw's top-level documentation describes "WhatsApp support" without
distinguishing API tier. Only reading their channel adapter source or searching
their dependency list reveals Baileys as the underlying library.

entries.title: Clinical REST endpoints must follow GCP-derived RBAC topology
entries.body:
Clinical REST endpoints must follow GCP-derived RBAC topology

Every REST endpoint in casehub-clinical must assign `@RolesAllowed` roles according to the ICH E6(R3) / 21 CFR Part 312 regulatory topology: SPONSOR owns trial-level management (create, activate, site selection, sponsor config); INVESTIGATOR and COORDINATOR own site-level clinical data entry (enroll, screen, AEs, deviations); INVESTIGATOR alone owns consent withdrawal (GDPR Art.17) and shares protocol amendment authority with SPONSOR; MONITOR (DSMB) has zero write access by regulatory independence mandate (ICH E6(R3) §5.18.3). All GET endpoints include all four roles. `quarkus.security.deny-unannotated-members=true` catches unannotated methods on annotated classes, but new resource classes with zero annotations are not covered — add `@RolesAllowed` to at least one method immediately.

entries.title: @IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs
entries.body:
@IfBuildProfile + @Alternative + @Priority resolves CDI without selected-alternatives in indexed JARs

### Symptom

Adding a dev-profile-only CDI bean (e.g., a `CurrentPrincipal` for dev mode) via `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` causes startup failures — all beans from the non-profiled `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` list disappear.

### Context

Quarkus profile-specific properties (`%dev.`, `%test.`, `%prod.`) completely **replace** their non-profiled counterparts. If the base `application.properties` has:

```properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=\
  com.example.JpaRepo,\
  com.example.JpaPlanItemStore,\
  com.example.JpaSubCaseGroupRepo
```

Adding `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.DevBean` drops all three JPA alternatives in dev mode — CDI startup fails with unsatisfied dependency errors for every bean that was in the original list.

### Root cause

Profile-specific properties in Quarkus are full replacements, not merges. This is by design (SmallRye Config behaviour), but the failure mode is silent — no warning that the non-profiled value was dropped.

### Fix

Use bean-level annotations instead of `selected-alternatives`:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
@Priority(150)  // beats OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)
@IfBuildProfile("dev")
public class DemoCurrentPrincipal implements CurrentPrincipal {
    @Override public String tenancyId() { return "demo-tenant"; }
    @Override public String actorId()   { return "demo-user"; }
}
```

**Requirements:**
1. The bean must live in a **Jandex-indexed JAR** (e.g., the application's own `runtime/` module). ArC discovers it automatically — no `selected-alternatives` entry needed.
2. `@IfBuildProfile("dev")` excludes the bean entirely from non-dev builds — no ambiguity in production.
3. `@Priority(150)` resolves over competing `@Alternative` beans (e.g., `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Priority(100)`) via standard ArC priority resolution.

This is the same mechanism by which `OidcCurrentPrincipal @Alternative @Priority(100)` beats `MockCurrentPrincipal @DefaultBean` in production — without being listed in `selected-alternatives`.

### Why this is non-obvious

The natural instinct is `%dev.quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=MyDevBean` — it looks like the standard profile-override pattern. The replacement behaviour is documented for SmallRye Config but not prominently flagged in the Quarkus CDI guide. The failure (dropped JPA alternatives) manifests as unrelated `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` errors that don't mention `selected-alternatives` at all.

### Why this fix

`@IfBuildProfile + @Priority` solves the problem at the bean level — no configuration property needed. The alternative (duplicating the full `selected-alternatives` list in every profile override) is fragile and drifts as new alternatives are added.

### Alternatives considered

- **Duplicate the full list in `%dev.`** — works but fragile; every new `@Alternative` bean must be added to every profile-specific override. Drifts silently.
- **Use `@UnlessBuildProfile("prod")` instead of `@IfBuildProfile("dev")`** — activates in both dev and test profiles, which may not be desired. Test profile has its own `FixedCurrentPrincipal @Priority(200)`.

entries.title: `RenameHandler` Intercepts Before `RenamePsiElementProcessor.substituteElementToRename()` Is Called
entries.body:
`RenameHandler` Intercepts Before `RenamePsiElementProcessor.substituteElementToRename()` Is Called

## `RenameHandler` Intercepts Before `RenamePsiElementProcessor.substituteElementToRename()` Is Called

**ID:** GE-0117
**Stack:** IntelliJ Platform SDK 232.x (2023.2), RenameHandler, RenamePsiElementProcessor

**Symptom:** `substituteElementToRename()` is never called when a custom `RenameHandler` is registered with high priority. Implementing `substituteElementToRename()` in a `RenamePsiElementProcessor` has no effect — the rename doesn't redirect as expected.

**Context:** Implementing a rename redirect: when the user renames a class in a generated file, `substituteElementToRename()` should silently redirect to the template class. A custom `RenameHandler` was already registered (with `order="first"`) to block renames on generated files. `substituteElementToRename()` was added to the processor but was never reached.

### Root cause
IntelliJ's rename pipeline has two layers:
1. `RenameHandler` — controls the overall UX flow (shows dialogs, blocks, etc.)
2. `RenamePsiElementProcessor` — participates in the rename once the handler decides to proceed

If a `RenameHandler.isAvailableOnDataContext()` returns `true`, IntelliJ uses that handler and calls `invoke()`. The standard rename flow — which calls `substituteElementToRename()` — never runs. The two extension points operate at different levels and don't compose automatically.

### Fix
Make the `RenameHandler` step aside for cases that `substituteElementToRename()` should handle. In `isAvailableOnDataContext()`, check if the element is one that the processor will redirect, and return `false` in that case:

```java
@Override
public boolean isAvailableOnDataContext(@NotNull DataContext dataContext) {
    VirtualFile file = CommonDataKeys.VIRTUAL_FILE.getData(dataContext);
    if (file == null || !isGeneratedFile(file)) return false;

// Step aside for elements that substituteElementToRename() will redirect
    Project project = CommonDataKeys.PROJECT.getData(dataContext);
    if (project != null && isHandledByProcessor(project, file)) return false;

return true; // show block dialog for everything else
}
```

### Why non-obvious
Both `RenameHandler` and `RenamePsiElementProcessor` are documented as rename extension points, but the documentation doesn't explain their execution order or that `RenameHandler` takes full control when available. A developer implementing `substituteElementToRename()` would expect it to always be called, not silently bypassed.

**See also:** GE-0079, GE-0080, GE-0081 (same rename processor pipeline)

*Score: 12/15 · Included because: completely non-obvious interaction between two documented extension points, silent failure · Reservation: specific to IntelliJ rename pipeline architecture*

entries.title: storeAll() must propagate SecurityException immediately — never collect it
entries.body:
storeAll() must propagate SecurityException immediately — never collect it

`CaseMemoryStore.storeAll()` overrides MUST re-throw `SecurityException` immediately when any `MemoryPermissions.assertTenant()` call fires. `SecurityException` is an authorization failure — it must never be swallowed or collected in `StoreAllResult.failures()`. Only backend `RuntimeException`s (network errors, DB failures, serialization exceptions) belong in `failures`. The distinction is enforced in the default implementation and in the CaseMemoryStoreSpiTest contract test. Adapters that do pre-flight tenant checks (assertTenant on all inputs before any backend call) must still throw when a check fails, even if prior inputs were already stored — the transactional contract (via JDBC or pre-flight) governs rollback, but the SecurityException itself always propagates to the caller.
