entries.title: Stateless multi-tier SLA escalation via candidateGroups — no state storage or decision serialization needed
Separate GRADE and OUTCOME in clinical AE memory entries
entries.body:
Separate GRADE and OUTCOME in clinical AE memory entries

AE memory entries written to the PATIENT domain must carry `ClinicalMemoryAttributes.GRADE` (CTCAE grade name, e.g. "GRADE_3") and `MemoryAttributeKeys.OUTCOME` (lifecycle status: "REPORTED", "ESCALATED", "DSMB_ESCALATED") as **separate attributes**. Conflating them — storing `grade.name()` as `OUTCOME` — breaks `hasPriorGrade3OrAbove()` for escalation entries and produces a facts map without a `grade` key, violating the spec contract at `.patientContext.facts[].grade`. The `ClinicalMemoryAttributes.GRADE` key is the canonical way to carry CTCAE grade through the memory store; `OUTCOME` is always a lifecycle or resolution status, never a grade.

entries.title: JPA InheritanceType.JOINED forces all hierarchy entities into one persistence unit
entries.body:
JPA InheritanceType.JOINED forces all hierarchy entities into one persistence unit

## Symptom

Migrating to named Hibernate persistence units fails with an error about missing entities or a persistence unit resolution error, even though the entities are correctly annotated. The issue only appears when entities from two different libraries share a `JOINED` inheritance hierarchy.

## Context

Occurs when: (1) migrating from the default Hibernate PU to a named PU, (2) entity `B extends A` with `@Inheritance(strategy = InheritanceType.JOINED)`, and (3) `A` and `B` live in different Maven modules or libraries.

## Root Cause

JPA requires all entities in an inheritance hierarchy to share a single persistence unit. If `A` is in `io.lib.model` and `B` is in `io.other.entity`, the named PU `packages` config must include both packages — even if you only want the `io.other` entities in that PU. Specifying only `io.other` causes Hibernate to fail because it can't resolve the full hierarchy.

## Fix

Include both packages in the named PU configuration:

```properties
quarkus.hibernate-orm.mypu.packages=io.other.entity,io.lib.model
```

This forces the parent entity's library into the same persistence unit, which may have schema implications (both libraries' tables are created/migrated by the same Flyway run).

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The constraint is a JPA specification requirement, but the error message from Quarkus/Hibernate does not say "hierarchy entities must share a PU." It typically presents as a mapping exception or missing entity error that points to the child entity, not to the inheritance relationship. The fix — including an external library's package in your PU config — feels wrong and is not mentioned in Quarkus named datasource documentation.

**See also:** GE-20260422-f86f42 — dual-PU setup when a library injects @Default EntityManager

**See also:** [GE-20260512-4d6f48](GE-20260512-4d6f48.md) — Panache entities cannot be scanned by two Quarkus persistence units simultaneously

entries.title: entityManager.persist() inside @PrePersist of a different entity is unsafe — JPA spec doesn't guarantee flush ordering
casehub-ledger LedgerProcessor build step blocks em.persist() on LedgerEntry subclasses — use LedgerEntryRepository.save()
entries.body:
casehub-ledger LedgerProcessor build step blocks em.persist() on LedgerEntry subclasses — use LedgerEntryRepository.save()

### Symptom
`IllegalStateException: LedgerEntry must be persisted through LedgerEntryRepository, which handles sequence allocation, enrichment, hashing, and signing. Direct em.persist() bypasses the entire save pipeline.`

Thrown at runtime when code calls `em.persist(entry)` on any `LedgerEntry` subclass (`CaseLedgerEntry`, `MessageLedgerEntry`, `WorkerDecisionEntry`, etc.).

### Context
A Quarkus extension using casehub-ledger, after upgrading to a SNAPSHOT that added a `LedgerProcessor` Quarkus build step. Any existing code that directly persisted `LedgerEntry` subclasses via `EntityManager.persist()` breaks with the above error.

### Root cause
`casehub-ledger` ships a `LedgerProcessor` Quarkus deployment build step (`validateDomainContentBytes`) that also adds a JPA `@PrePersist` interceptor to every `LedgerEntry` subclass. The interceptor throws `IllegalStateException` if it detects a direct persist (i.e. the entity was not saved through the `LedgerEntryRepository` save pipeline).

The intent: `LedgerEntryRepository.save()` handles sequence number allocation (via `ledger_subject_sequence` table), leaf hash computation, Merkle chain update, and agent signature — bypassing it produces invalid ledger entries.

### Fix
Replace `em.persist(entry)` + `em.flush()` with `ledgerRepo.save(entry, tenancyId)`:

```java
// ❌ Blocked since casehub-ledger SNAPSHOT (will throw at runtime):
em.persist(entry);
em.flush();

// ✅ Correct:
@Inject LedgerEntryRepository ledgerRepo;
// ...
ledgerRepo.save(entry, tenancyId);
// save() handles sequence allocation, Merkle hash, and signing; no manual flush needed.
```

Also remove `@LedgerPersistenceUnit EntityManager em` injection if it was only used for `persist()` + `flush()` and the `nextSequenceNumber()` query (the repo handles sequencing internally).

### Why this is non-obvious
The `IllegalStateException` message is clear once seen, but:
1. The validation is added at Quarkus augmentation time via a deployment build step — it does not appear in the `LedgerEntryRepository` interface Javadoc or any user-facing docs.
2. Code that was previously working (`em.persist()`) breaks silently if the SNAPSHOT is updated but the calling code is not.
3. The `@PrePersist` interceptor is injected by the Quarkus extension's deployment artifact (`casehub-ledger-deployment`) — the mechanism is invisible when reading the entity class.

### Why this fix
`LedgerEntryRepository.save()` is the only supported path because it atomically allocates the per-subject sequence number from `ledger_subject_sequence`, computes the Merkle leaf hash, and updates the Merkle frontier — operations that must be coordinated to maintain chain integrity. Any bypass produces a ledger entry with an incorrect or missing sequence number and hash.

### Caveats
- `LedgerEntryRepository.save()` triggers a full Merkle chain update — see GE-20260531-d2ed26 for concurrent-write implications.
- The `ledger_subject_sequence` table must exist in the database. In test contexts using `hibernate.database.generation=drop-and-create`, this native SQL table is not created from JPA entities — use `quarkus.hibernate-orm.<pu-name>.sql-load-script` to create it (see also GE-20260612-*).

**See also:** [GE-20260612-de141c](GE-20260612-de141c.md) — casehub-ledger LedgerProcessor build step requires domainContentBytes() on all LedgerEntry subclasses with @Column fields

entries.title: Three-tier Maven module structure — api/common/runtime
entries.body:
Three-tier Maven module structure — api/common/runtime

## Three-tier Maven module structure — api/common/runtime

**Topic:** Multi-module Maven structure for Quarkus extensions
**Alternatives exist:** yes — flat single-module, two-tier api/runtime, monolithic runtime

### The convention

Three tiers, ordered lightest to heaviest:

**Tier 1 — `api/` (pure Java):** Interfaces, records, enums, POJOs. No Quarkus, no CDI, no JPA. Any Java app can consume this tier regardless of framework.

**Tier 2 — `common/` or `core/` (CDI, no JPA):** Shared helpers and CDI beans. No JPA entities, no Flyway migrations, no datasource dependency.

**Tier 3 — `runtime/`, `engine/`, `app/` (full Quarkus):** JPA entities, Flyway migrations, REST resources, Quarkus config — everything.

Dependency direction: Tier 3 → Tier 2 → Tier 1. Never reversed.

### Why this style

JPA entities force every consumer to configure a datasource. CDI annotations in a Tier 1 module force all consumers to have Quarkus. Separating the three tiers means lightweight consumers (other pure-Java libs, tests using in-memory repos) can depend only on `api/` with zero runtime overhead and no datasource requirement.

### Trade-offs

More modules to navigate. SPIs that genuinely need external SDK types (Vert.x, Hibernate Reactive) must live in a tier that already depends on that SDK — this sometimes surprises contributors who add Quarkus types to `api/` then wonder why consumers break.

### When not to use it

Single-module projects or projects where all consumers are full Quarkus apps and a datasource-in-tests is acceptable across the board.

*Score: 12/15 · Included because: JPA/CDI contamination across module tiers is non-obvious; the three-tier boundary makes it structural rather than disciplinary · Reservation: none*

**See also:** [GE-20260515-ffde26](GE-20260515-ffde26.md) — optional Quarkus features: Jandex library module pattern

**See also:** [GE-20260501-b88737](GE-20260501-b88737.md) — parallel SPI interfaces in api/ and runtime/ diverge silently

entries.title: GitHub Actions cache/restore + cache/save as separate v4 actions enables failure-aware state persistence
entries.body:
GitHub Actions cache/restore + cache/save as separate v4 actions enables failure-aware state persistence

## What it is

Use `actions/cache/restore@v4` and `actions/cache/save@v4` as separate steps rather than the combined `actions/cache@v4`. This gives explicit control over *when* the cache is written and *what* is written — independent of whether the job succeeded or failed.

## When to use it

Any workflow where the cache contents should depend on per-step outcomes rather than overall job success. The canonical case: an incremental build that tracks which modules built successfully. Failed modules should NOT have their SHA recorded (so the next run retries them); succeeded modules should.

## The pattern

```yaml
# Restore at the start — gets the most recent matching cache
- uses: actions/cache/restore@v4
  with:
    path: .incremental-state
    key: state-${{ github.ref_name }}-${{ github.run_id }}
    restore-keys: |
      state-${{ github.ref_name }}-

# ... build steps run, some succeed, some fail ...

# Save at the end with if: always() — runs even when steps failed
# Write only the SHA for modules that succeeded; keep previous SHA for failures
- name: Save state
  if: always()
  run: |
    record() {
      local name=$1 cur=$2 outcome=$3 prev=$4
      [ "$outcome" = "success" ] && echo "${name}=${cur}" || echo "${name}=${prev}"
    }
    { record ledger "$SHA_L" "$OUTCOME_L" "$PREV_L"; ... } > .incremental-state/shas.txt

- uses: actions/cache/save@v4
  if: always()
  with:
    path: .incremental-state
    key: state-${{ github.ref_name }}-${{ github.run_id }}
```

## Why the combined action can't do this

`actions/cache@v4` auto-saves in a post-step that runs after the job completes. You cannot control what is written to disk before that post-step runs based on individual step outcomes — by then, all steps have finished. The separate save action runs as an explicit step where you have full access to `steps.<id>.outcome` and can decide exactly what state to persist.

## Why non-obvious

The GitHub Actions docs present `actions/cache` as the standard pattern. The separate restore/save actions are documented but presented as advanced usage. The motivation for splitting them — outcome-dependent state — is not mentioned in the docs; it's only obvious once you've hit the limitation of the combined action in an incremental build scenario.

**See also:** [GE-20260524-e0aabf](GE-20260524-e0aabf.md) — module missing from state-save step always rebuilds on next run

entries.title: JPA @PrePersist vs explicit enricher call — dual-persistence implementations silently diverge on hash coverage
entries.body:
JPA @PrePersist vs explicit enricher call — dual-persistence implementations silently diverge on hash coverage

## JPA @PrePersist vs explicit enricher call — dual-persistence implementations silently diverge on hash coverage

### Symptom

Hash digests computed by the JPA path do not include data set by enrichers
(e.g., provenance supplements), while the in-memory path's digests do. The
divergence is invisible — tests don't cross-verify hashes between implementations.

### Context

Two `LedgerEntryRepository` implementations exist:

**JPA path** (`JpaLedgerEntryRepository.save()`):
```
1. stamp fields, allocate seqNum
2. digest = leafHash(entry)           ← BEFORE enrichers
3. em.persist() → @PrePersist → enricher pipeline runs
```

**In-memory path** (`InMemoryLedgerEntryRepository.save()`):
```
1. stamp fields, allocate seqNum
2. enricherPipeline.enrich(entry)     ← BEFORE digest
3. digest = leafHash(entry)
```

The JPA path computes the digest at step 2, then `em.persist()` triggers
`@PrePersist` which runs enrichers at step 3. The in-memory path runs
enrichers first (step 2), then computes the digest (step 3). Enricher-set
data (provenance supplements, traceId) is in the in-memory digest but not
the JPA digest.

### Root cause

The JPA `@PrePersist` lifecycle callback fires DURING `em.persist()`, not
before it. The save method computed the digest before calling `em.persist()`,
so the enricher pipeline — triggered by `@PrePersist` — ran after the digest
was already locked in. The in-memory path had no such constraint and correctly
ran enrichers before hashing.

### Fix

Move enrichment out of `@PrePersist` into an explicit call in `save()`, before
digest computation. Both paths then follow the same ordering:
enrich → hash → persist.

```java
// JPA save() — fixed ordering
enricherPipeline.enrich(entry);                    // explicit call
entry.digest = LedgerMerkleTree.leafHash(entry);   // hash AFTER enrichment
em.persist(entry);                                  // no enrichers in @PrePersist
```

Convert `@PrePersist` listener to a defensive check that throws if an entry
reaches `em.persist()` without a digest.

### Why this is non-obvious

Both implementations produce valid-looking hashes. The divergence is only
detectable by recomputing the hash from the persisted entry and comparing —
which no test did because each implementation was tested in isolation. The
in-memory path was accidentally correct.

results.query_text: ConcurrentHashMap|CopyOnWriteArrayList|thread.safe|lock.free

entries.title: GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing
entries.body:
GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing

## Behaviour

`GreenMailExtension` extends `GreenMailProxy`. In `greenmail-junit5 2.1.3`, `GreenMailProxy.getGreenMail()` is declared `protected`:

```bash
$ javap -classpath greenmail-junit5-2.1.3.jar com.icegreen.greenmail.junit5.GreenMailExtension
...
protected com.icegreen.greenmail.util.GreenMail getGreenMail();
```

Attempting to call it from test code:

```java
GreenMail mail = GREEN_MAIL.getGreenMail(); // compile error: getGreenMail() has protected access
```

## Why This Matters

`GreenMail` (the bare class) has a `deliver(MimeMessage)` method and other useful internal APIs. The extension does not expose them. Since `getGreenMail()` is protected, you cannot retrieve the underlying instance to call them.

**Workaround:** In `@QuarkusTestResource` implementations that construct `GreenMail` directly (not via the extension), you hold the instance yourself as a static field — giving you full access to `deliver()`, `getManagers()`, etc.

## Why It's Undocumented

The Greenmail documentation and README make no mention of this access restriction. The Greenmail JUnit 5 guide shows the extension as a drop-in for the JUnit 4 Rule and implies equivalent functionality. The only way to discover the `protected` modifier is via `javap` on the jar.

**See also:** [GE-20260529-aa8445](GE-20260529-aa8445.md) — GreenMailExtension has no public deliver() method

entries.title: Security Audit — Principles
entries.body:
**Rate limiting** - prevent DoS attacks:
- Implement rate limiting on authentication endpoints
- Throttle expensive operations
- Use backpressure for queue processing

**Least privilege** - minimize permissions:
- Run services with minimal required permissions
- Separate read/write database users
- Use fine-grained permission models

**Audit logging** - track security-relevant events:
- Log authentication attempts (success and failure)
- Log authorization failures
- Log access to sensitive data
- Include user ID, timestamp, action, resource

---

## Severity Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Vulnerability_detected((Vulnerability detected))
    Exploitable_remotely_{Exploitable remotely?}
    Leads_to_data_breach_or_RCE_{Leads to data breach or RCE?}
    Requires_authentication_{Requires authentication?}
    CRITICAL[CRITICAL]
    Defense_in_depth_issue_{Defense in depth issue?}
    WARNING[WARNING]
    NOTE[NOTE]
    Vulnerability_detected --> Exploitable_remotely_
    Exploitable_remotely_ -->|yes| Leads_to_data_breach_or_RCE_
    Exploitable_remotely_ -->|no| Defense_in_depth_issue_
    Leads_to_data_breach_or_RCE_ -->|yes| CRITICAL
    Leads_to_data_breach_or_RCE_ -->|no| Requires_authentication_
    Requires_authentication_ -->|yes| WARNING
    Requires_authentication_ -->|no| CRITICAL
    Defense_in_depth_issue_ -->|yes| WARNING
    Defense_in_depth_issue_ -->|no| NOTE
```

---

## Common Pitfalls

| Mistake | Why It's Wrong | Fix |
|---------|----------------|-----|
| "It's only accessible to authenticated users" | Auth can be bypassed, always validate authorization | Check permissions even for authenticated endpoints |
| "Input validation on frontend" | Frontend can be bypassed | Always validate on backend |
| "This endpoint isn't public" | Security through obscurity fails | Protect all endpoints |
| "We'll fix it after launch" | Vulnerabilities get exploited quickly | Fix before production |
| Trusting environment variables blindly | Env vars can be exposed or leaked | Validate and sanitize env var contents |
| Using blacklist validation | Attackers find ways around blacklists | Use whitelist validation |
| Logging sensitive data for debugging | Logs leak to unauthorized parties | Never log passwords, tokens, PII |
| "Nobody knows this endpoint exists" | Attackers scan and enumerate | Assume all endpoints will be discovered |

---

## See Also

- `~/.hortora/garden/approaches/code-review.md` — General code review principles (safety, concurrency, testing)

entries.title: quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false disables @RolesAllowed enforcement in dev without a real OIDC server
entries.body:
quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false disables @RolesAllowed enforcement in dev without a real OIDC server

# quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false disables @RolesAllowed enforcement in dev — allows OIDC service to run without a real OIDC server

### Context
Running a Quarkus service with `quarkus-oidc` and `@RolesAllowed` annotations in dev mode
without a real OIDC server (no DevServices, no Keycloak). All guarded endpoints return 401
for every request.

### Technique
Set in `application.properties`:
```properties
%dev.quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false
%dev.quarkus.oidc.enabled=false
%dev.quarkus.keycloak.devservices.enabled=false
```

`quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false` activates `DevModeDisabledAuthorizationController`
(`@Alternative @Priority @Singleton` in `quarkus-security-runtime-spi`), which returns `false`
from `isAuthorizationEnabled()`. Both `EagerSecurityHandler` (REST path — checks at line 53
before processing the request) and `StandardSecurityCheckInterceptor` (CDI path — checks via
`controller.isAuthorizationEnabled()`) bail out early. `@RolesAllowed` is not enforced.

`quarkus.oidc.enabled=false` is also required — without it, OIDC intercepts requests before
the authorization check fires, returning 401 for missing tokens even with auth disabled.

### Why this is non-obvious
The natural dev-mode approaches are: (a) spin up Keycloak DevServices (60–90s cold start,
heavyweight), or (b) configure a real OIDC server. `quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode`
is not mentioned in the main OIDC documentation; it appears only in `quarkus-security-runtime-spi`
source as the activation mechanism for `DevModeDisabledAuthorizationController`. The interceptor
mechanism is two-level — disabling OIDC alone is insufficient because the authorization
interceptor fires independently of the authentication mechanism.

### Why this fix
Over disabling DevServices alone: `quarkus.keycloak.devservices.enabled=false` without the
auth flag still returns 401 — the security framework enforces `@RolesAllowed` against anonymous
identity regardless of whether an auth mechanism is registered. The auth flag is the only clean
way to bypass the enforcement layer itself.

entries.title: casehub-qhorus MessageService methods using Panache static calls bypass InMemory store in @QuarkusTest
Lock-outside-transaction pattern for safe concurrent sequence assignment in CDI/JTA
Use @PermitAll (not unannotated) for intentionally public endpoints when adding a security framework to an existing app
ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet().add() provides atomic idempotent registration without explicit locks
entries.body:
ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet().add() provides atomic idempotent registration without explicit locks

### Context

You need to register a resource (e.g. a backend in a registry, a channel in a gateway, a listener on a topic) exactly once per key, even under concurrent access from multiple threads. You want to avoid explicit locks, `synchronized` blocks, and the awkward semantics of `computeIfAbsent` when the computation has side effects.

### Technique

```java
private final Set<UUID> registeredKeys = ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet();

public void ensureRegistered(UUID key) {
    if (registeredKeys.add(key)) {   // atomic: exactly one thread returns true
        doRegistration(key);          // called exactly once per key
    }
}

public void deregister(UUID key) {
    registeredKeys.remove(key);      // subsequent ensureRegistered() will re-register
}
```

`ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet()` returns a `Set<K>` backed by a ConcurrentHashMap. `Set.add()` returns `true` if the element was actually added (was absent) and `false` if it was already present. Under concurrent access, exactly one thread gets `true` — all others get `false` and skip the side effect. No explicit locking required.

### Why this is non-obvious

Most developers reach for `synchronized(this)` or `computeIfAbsent`. The `newKeySet().add()` pattern:
- Is 2 lines vs 5-10 for a `synchronized` block
- Returns the boolean signal the caller needs directly from `add()`
- Has guaranteed CAS semantics from the ConcurrentHashMap backing
- Clearly expresses intent: "register this key once, skip if already registered"

`computeIfAbsent` also works but its Javadoc warns that the computation must not modify the map — using it to trigger side effects (registration) is semantically awkward and potentially unsafe if the side effect is not pure. The `Set.add()` pattern has no such restriction.

### Constraints

The side effect in `doRegistration()` must be safe to call after `add()` returns but before other threads observe the registration in the set. If `doRegistration()` itself must be atomic with the `add()` (e.g. the registration step reads back the key), this pattern is insufficient — use a dedicated lock or a more explicit state machine.

entries.title: quarkus.http.auth.permission.*.policy=deny on /* blocks ALL requests — @RolesAllowed becomes unreachable dead code
entries.body:
quarkus.http.auth.permission.*.policy=deny on /* blocks ALL requests — @RolesAllowed becomes unreachable dead code

### Symptom
Every endpoint returns 403. Always. Regardless of authentication status, role, or `@RolesAllowed` annotation. The application is completely non-functional.

### Context
When adding OIDC + `@RolesAllowed` to a Quarkus application, a natural instinct is to add a catch-all deny policy so missing annotations fail closed:
```properties
quarkus.http.auth.permission.default.paths=/*
quarkus.http.auth.permission.default.policy=deny
```

### What was tried (didn't work)
The above configuration. Also tried adding `/q/*` health exemptions — the deny policy still blocks all application endpoints.

### Root cause
`quarkus.http.auth.permission.*` policies operate at the **Vert.x HTTP filter layer** — before the JAX-RS container processes the request and before CDI security interceptors run. A `deny` policy on `/*` rejects all requests at that filter, regardless of authentication status, role, or any `@RolesAllowed` annotation. `@RolesAllowed` is never evaluated — it is unreachable dead code.

Additionally, `quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false` (which suppresses `@RolesAllowed` evaluation in dev mode) does **not** suppress HTTP permission policies — those are at the `io.quarkus.vertx.http.runtime.security` layer, not the CDI authorization controller layer. Dev mode is also completely broken.

### Fix
Do not use `quarkus.http.auth.permission.*.policy=deny` as a catch-all. Use the JAX-RS-layer mechanism instead:
```properties
quarkus.security.deny-unannotated-members=true
```
This operates inside the JAX-RS container after authentication. `@RolesAllowed` annotations remain fully functional. It is suppressed in dev mode by `auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false` (same CDI authorization controller layer).

### Why this is non-obvious
The two security layers (Vert.x HTTP filter vs CDI/JAX-RS interceptor) are not clearly distinguished in the Quarkus documentation. Both use `quarkus.` config prefixes. The HTTP permission approach looks like a standard deny-by-default pattern and produces no startup warning — the 403s only appear at runtime.

results.query_text: Thread-safe in-memory store using ConcurrentHashMap and CopyOnWriteArrayList for lock-free concurrent access with READ COMMITTED semantics

entries.title: Lock-outside-transaction pattern for safe concurrent sequence assignment in CDI/JTA
Read-modify-write on thread-safe store is not atomic — concurrent writers lose updates
nextSequenceNumber() row lock incidentally serializes the Merkle frontier update for same-subject concurrent saves
entries.body:
nextSequenceNumber() row lock incidentally serializes the Merkle frontier update for same-subject concurrent saves

### What Was Discovered

`JpaLedgerEntryRepository.save()` is `@Transactional`. The first DB write it makes is `LedgerSequenceAllocator.nextSequenceNumber()`, which executes:

```sql
INSERT INTO ledger_subject_sequence (subject_id, next_seq)
VALUES (CAST(?1 AS UUID), 1)
ON CONFLICT (subject_id) DO UPDATE SET next_seq = ledger_subject_sequence.next_seq + 1
```

In PostgreSQL, this `INSERT ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE` acquires a write lock on the `ledger_subject_sequence` row for the given `subject_id`. That lock is held for the **entire duration** of the enclosing `@Transactional` transaction — which includes the Merkle frontier read-compute-replace cycle that comes later in the same `save()` call.

**Effect:** A second concurrent `save()` for the same `subject_id` blocks on the `INSERT ON CONFLICT` until the first transaction commits. By the time the second transaction proceeds, the first has committed its frontier update. The second reads the committed frontier and computes the correct next Merkle state.

This means the Merkle chain is **correct for concurrent JPA saves on the same subject** — not because of any explicit frontier locking, but because the sequence allocation lock incidentally serializes the entire pipeline.

### Why This Is Non-Obvious

The Merkle frontier update (`findBySubjectId → append → replace`) is a read-modify-write cycle with no `SELECT FOR UPDATE`. Inspection of the code suggests a race condition: two concurrent transactions could both read the same stale frontier and produce inconsistent chains. In practice, this race does not occur on PostgreSQL because the upstream sequence lock serializes them. The connection between sequence allocation locking and Merkle frontier safety is not documented anywhere in the codebase.

### The Merkle Serialization Invariant

Three facts must simultaneously hold for the implicit serialization to remain valid:

1. **`save()` is `@Transactional`** — the sequence lock must cover the frontier update.
2. **`nextSequenceNumber()` is the first per-subject serializing DB write in `save()`** — the lock must be acquired before the frontier read. Other writes (e.g. `actor_identity` tokenization for `ActorType.HUMAN`) may precede it but are per-actor, not per-subject.
3. **`JpaLedgerMerkleFrontierRepository.replace()` participates in the same transaction** — via `@Transactional(REQUIRED)` propagation.

If any of these changes — `save()` split into two transactions, sequence allocation moved to `REQUIRES_NEW`, `replace()` made `NOT_SUPPORTED` — the Merkle chain silently corrupts without any test or constraint catching it.

### InMemory Path

`InMemoryLedgerEntryRepository` does not benefit from this implicit serialization. It must use an explicit per-subject `synchronized` block covering sequence allocation through frontier update.
