entries.title: Parallel agents with domain-split substitution tables for large-scale consistent project-wide renames
entries.body:
Parallel agents with domain-split substitution tables for large-scale consistent project-wide renames

## What Developers Normally Do

Write sed scripts or use IDE bulk find-replace to rename project-wide symbols.
For renames with exclusion rules (e.g. "rename our package prefix but not
third-party deps that share the same prefix"), sed scripts require complex
negative lookaheads and still miss contextual distinctions. IDE replace-all
has no concept of "ignore this pattern when it appears in a dependency
declaration."

## The Technique

Split files by domain concern and dispatch one parallel agent per domain,
each given an explicit substitution table and explicit "DO NOT change" rules:

```
Agent 1: Java source files
  — substitution table: quarkus-ledger → casehub-ledger, etc.
  — exclusion rule: do NOT change io.quarkiverse.X where X is not our extension

Agent 2: Core documentation (DESIGN.md, integration-guide.md, etc.)
  — same substitution table

Agent 3: Historical plan/spec docs, root files, HANDOFF
  — same substitution table

Agent 4: Blog entries (frontmatter), examples, settings files
  — same substitution table
```

Use `isolation: "worktree"` on each agent. Changes auto-commit and merge back
into main. Run a final grep verification pass to catch any stragglers.

```
Agent({ isolation: "worktree", prompt: """
  Fix these exact substitutions in Java files only:
    quarkus-ledger → casehub-ledger
    io.quarkiverse.ledger → io.casehub.ledger (ONLY when it's our package, NOT third-party)
  Exclusion: do NOT change io.quarkiverse.opentelemetry or any other third-party quarkiverse lib.
  Final verification: grep to confirm zero remaining matches.
"""})
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The instinct is to write sed scripts or use IDE bulk replace. Three things
make the agent approach superior for renames with exclusion rules:

1. **Contextual exclusion** — agents read the surrounding code and can
   distinguish `io.quarkiverse.ledger` (our old package) from
   `io.quarkiverse.opentelemetry` (a third-party dep). sed requires brittle
   negative lookaheads that break on unforeseen patterns.

2. **Domain splitting** — splitting by file type (Java / docs / plans / blog)
   rather than by feature lets each agent operate on a homogeneous set with
   a consistent grammar, reducing false positives.

3. **Implicit validation** — asking each agent to run a final grep
   verification at the end catches its own misses without a separate pass.

The parallel dispatch (single message, four Agent tool calls) keeps the total
elapsed time similar to a single sed pass, while producing more reliable
results on a 60+ file rename with non-trivial exclusion rules.

**See also:** GE-0166 (parallel agents for codebase comparison), GE-20260420-374a25 (parallel implementation subagents for disjoint tasks)

**See also:** [GE-20260420-374a25](GE-20260420-374a25.md) — dispatch parallel implementation subagents within subagent-driven-development

entries.title: Declare ARC42STORIES.MD as primary architecture record at bootstrap — LAYER-LOG.md becomes the source-of-truth draft
entries.body:
Declare ARC42STORIES.MD as primary architecture record at bootstrap — LAYER-LOG.md becomes the source-of-truth draft

When a CaseHub harness app bootstraps `ARC42STORIES.MD`, update `CLAUDE.md` in the same commit to declare `ARC42STORIES.MD` as the primary architecture record and `LAYER-LOG.md` as the source-of-truth draft that feeds it. Also add redirect headers to `docs/DESIGN.md` and workspace `DESIGN.md` pointing to `ARC42STORIES.MD` sections. Do not retire or delete `LAYER-LOG.md` until `ARC42STORIES.MD` has been reviewed and verified complete against all source documents (quality gate: `arc42stories-post-generation-quality-gate.md`). The reference implementation is `casehubio/clinical#54` commit `581b439`.

entries.title: ARC42STORIES.MD lives in the project repo, not the workspace
entries.body:
ARC42STORIES.MD lives in the project repo, not the workspace

ARC42STORIES.MD is the architecture record for a CaseHub application — equivalent to DESIGN.md before the migration. Like DESIGN.md, it belongs in the project repo root (e.g. `casehubio/devtown`), not the workspace. The workspace holds methodology artifacts (handovers, blog, plans, snapshots); architectural documentation belongs alongside the code where contributors can read it. When the migration from DESIGN.md to ARC42STORIES.MD happened, several repos accidentally placed the file in the workspace — audit with: find the file under `~/claude/casehub/<repo>/` (project) vs `~/claude/public/casehub/<repo>/` (workspace).

entries.title: Run three-check quality sweep before closing ARC42STORIES.MD migration issue
entries.body:
Run three-check quality sweep before closing ARC42STORIES.MD migration issue

After generating ARC42STORIES.MD from LAYER-LOG.md, three checks are mandatory before closing the migration issue. (1) Run `gh issue view N --repo casehubio/REPO` for every issue reference in §12 — closed issues must be removed from Active Risks; (2) Run `find . -name "ClassName.java"` for every class in §9.4 Key files sections — class names from design docs may not exist in production; (3) Open each service implementation and verify CDI annotations match the document — `@ApplicationScoped` may have evolved to `@Alternative @Priority(N)` when competing implementations were introduced. The devtown migration found 10+ documentation errors using these three checks. See garden entries GE-20260601-b0eabf, GE-20260601-c09f71, GE-20260601-85afd0 for the specific gotchas each check catches.

entries.title: Use conventional commit scope as the primary feature-clustering signal over file paths
entries.body:
Use conventional commit scope as the primary feature-clustering signal over file paths

## Use conventional commit scope as the primary feature-clustering signal over file paths

**ID:** GE-0043
**Stack:** Git (any version), conventional commits format
**Labels:** `#git` `#analysis` `#strategy`
**What it achieves:** Groups commits into feature-area clusters that match the author's own intent, producing far more meaningful issue groupings than file-path analysis — especially for repos where temporal signals are absent.
**Context:** When analysing a git history to group commits into features, epics, or tickets. Most applicable when the repo uses conventional commits (`feat(scope): description`).

### The technique

Extract the scope from the conventional commit subject line:

```python
import re
from collections import defaultdict

def extract_scope(subject: str) -> str | None:
    """Extract 'feat(garden): ...' → 'garden'. None if no scope."""
    m = re.match(r"^[a-z]+\(([^)]+)\):", subject)
    return m.group(1) if m else None

scope_clusters: dict[str | None, list] = defaultdict(list)
for commit in commits:
    scope_clusters[extract_scope(commit.subject)].append(commit)

# scope_clusters["garden"] → all feat/fix/docs(garden) commits together
# scope_clusters[None]     → commits with no scope → fall back to file path
```

Fall back to top-level directory for commits with no scope:

```python
def primary_area(subject: str, changed_files: list[str]) -> str:
    scope = extract_scope(subject)
    if scope:
        return scope
    if changed_files:
        return changed_files[0].split("/")[0]
    return "misc"
```

### Why this is non-obvious

The natural first instinct is to use file paths — "what did this commit touch?" — because file paths are always available. But top-level directories in many repos (`docs/`, `tests/`, `scripts/`) are cross-cutting infrastructure, not features. They dominate by volume but carry no feature signal.

The commit scope, by contrast, is the author explicitly labelling which feature they were working on. `feat(garden)` and `fix(garden)` both belong to the garden feature even if they touch different directories. The scope is the single strongest clustering signal available in conventional-commit repos.

Validated on a 279-commit repo: top-level dirs gave `docs` (114 hits), `tests` (83), `scripts` (48) — useless. Scopes gave `marketplace` (33), `garden` (12), `write-blog` (11) — exactly the feature areas.

### When to use it

- Grouping commits into features/epics for retrospective issue creation
- Building git history analysis tools (changelog generators, scope visualisers)
- Any workflow that needs to partition commits without reading diffs (faster, less API cost)
- Fallback to directory is safe for repos without consistent scoping

*Score: 12/15 · Included because: counter-intuitive that author-intent labels beat file-system signals; validated empirically on a real repo with before/after numbers · Reservation: only applies to repos using conventional commits*

entries.title: Disable squash/rebase merges across all org repos in one API loop
entries.body:
Disable squash/rebase merges across all org repos in one API loop

## Context

A GitHub organisation has accumulated repos where squash merges are enabled,
causing history loss. You want to enforce a consistent merge policy (no squash,
rebase allowed) across all repos at once.

## Technique

Use `gh api --method PATCH` in a loop over all repos:

```bash
for repo in engine ledger work qhorus claudony parent; do
  gh api --method PATCH repos/myorg/$repo \
    -f allow_squash_merge=false \
    -f allow_rebase_merge=true \
    -f allow_merge_commit=true \
    --jq '{repo:.full_name, squash:.allow_squash_merge, rebase:.allow_rebase_merge}'
done
```

The `--jq` flag confirms the new settings in the output without a separate
verification step. To also enable auto-delete of merged branches:
```bash
-f delete_branch_on_merge=true
```

## Why this is non-obvious

GitHub's UI requires navigating to each repo's Settings → General → Pull Requests
section individually. There is no bulk org-level policy enforcement in the UI for
merge strategy. The API approach takes seconds for any number of repos and produces
a verifiable output row per repo.

**See also:** GE-20260426-61854e — create GitHub org team and grant write access via gh CLI

entries.title: Mode-first generation is the anti-slop fix — wrong mode is the root cause, banned words are a symptom
entries.body:
Mode-first generation is the anti-slop fix — wrong mode is the root cause, banned words are a symptom

## What happens

Anti-slop guidance accumulates banned words and structural patterns ("no delve", "no 'it's important to note'"). Despite the lists, AI-sounding prose keeps appearing. Adding more banned words doesn't solve it. The lists treat symptoms.

## Root cause

LLMs default to the prose end of the structure–prose spectrum due to statistical pressure from training corpora dominated by prose. When asked to write a Gotcha section, the LLM generates explanation-mode prose because that's what the training distribution pulls toward — not because it's banned-word-free.

## The technique

Identify the required mode before generating. Generate to that mode's structural constraints. The mode determines the structure; the structure prevents the banned words from appearing.

- How-to/diagnostic mode → Symptom → Cause → Fix (forced structure prevents flowing prose)
- Reference/inventory mode → `path — one sentence` (forces telegraphic format)
- Tutorial mode → numbered imperative steps (forces sequential, not narrative)

If the mode is wrong, fixing individual word choices is fighting downstream effects.

## Why non-obvious

The instinct is to add more banned words to anti-slop lists. The non-obvious fix is to go upstream: identify the mode required by the content, then constrain the generation to that mode's structural form. The banned words become self-enforcing side effects of correct mode selection.

**Validated empirically:** arc42stories sections with explicit structural prescriptions (Symptom → Cause → Fix) stayed in mode across every implementation. Sections with only narrative instructions ("teaching narrative, explain what this layer adds") drifted to prose. Same authors, same tool, same document — the structural prescription was the only differentiator.

### Why this fix

The prose default is a distributional artifact, not a vocabulary problem. Constraining structure at the generation prompt level overrides the distributional pull before it has any effect. Word-level filtering operates on the output of a wrong-mode generation and can never fully compensate.

entries.title: Clinical REST endpoints must follow GCP-derived RBAC topology
entries.body:
Clinical REST endpoints must follow GCP-derived RBAC topology

Every REST endpoint in casehub-clinical must assign `@RolesAllowed` roles according to the ICH E6(R3) / 21 CFR Part 312 regulatory topology: SPONSOR owns trial-level management (create, activate, site selection, sponsor config); INVESTIGATOR and COORDINATOR own site-level clinical data entry (enroll, screen, AEs, deviations); INVESTIGATOR alone owns consent withdrawal (GDPR Art.17) and shares protocol amendment authority with SPONSOR; MONITOR (DSMB) has zero write access by regulatory independence mandate (ICH E6(R3) §5.18.3). All GET endpoints include all four roles. `quarkus.security.deny-unannotated-members=true` catches unannotated methods on annotated classes, but new resource classes with zero annotations are not covered — add `@RolesAllowed` to at least one method immediately.

results.query_text: In-memory store aggregate methods must not delegate to scan because scan applies pagination which silently truncates aggregation results

entries.title: Separate GRADE and OUTCOME in clinical AE memory entries
entries.body:
Separate GRADE and OUTCOME in clinical AE memory entries

AE memory entries written to the PATIENT domain must carry `ClinicalMemoryAttributes.GRADE` (CTCAE grade name, e.g. "GRADE_3") and `MemoryAttributeKeys.OUTCOME` (lifecycle status: "REPORTED", "ESCALATED", "DSMB_ESCALATED") as **separate attributes**. Conflating them — storing `grade.name()` as `OUTCOME` — breaks `hasPriorGrade3OrAbove()` for escalation entries and produces a facts map without a `grade` key, violating the spec contract at `.patientContext.facts[].grade`. The `ClinicalMemoryAttributes.GRADE` key is the canonical way to carry CTCAE grade through the memory store; `OUTCOME` is always a lifecycle or resolution status, never a grade.

entries.title: @QuarkusTest classes sharing the same H2 in-memory JDBC URL contaminate each other's data
entries.body:
@QuarkusTest classes sharing the same H2 in-memory JDBC URL contaminate each other's data

## Symptom

A `@QuarkusTest` class that queries all rows (e.g., `listAll()`, `findAll()`) returns more rows than expected — rows inserted by a *different* test class that ran earlier in the same test suite execution.

Tests pass in isolation (`-Dtest=MyTest`) but fail when the full suite runs.

## Context

Occurs when:
- Multiple `@QuarkusTest` classes use the same H2 in-memory URL (e.g., `jdbc:h2:mem:mydb;DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1`)
- One test class inserts rows without cleaning up
- A later test class runs a `listAll()`-style query that returns all rows

H2's `mem:` databases persist for the lifetime of the JVM when `DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1` is set. All `@QuarkusTest` classes that share the same URL see the same database instance — including each other's leftover data.

## Root Cause

`DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1` keeps the H2 in-memory database alive as long as the JVM runs. Quarkus reuses the same application context across `@QuarkusTest` classes in the same surefire fork (unless a test requires a new context via `@QuarkusTestProfile`). Both conditions together mean test data accumulates.

Quarkus's `@TestTransaction` annotation rolls back transactions per test method but applies only to the annotated test class. Rows committed by other test classes without `@TestTransaction` persist across all subsequent tests.

## Fix

Give the test class that needs isolation its own H2 database URL via a profile property:

```properties
# In src/test/resources/application.properties — only affects the named profile
%retention-test.quarkus.datasource.jdbc.url=jdbc:h2:mem:retentiontestdb;DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1
```

Activate the profile on the test class:
```java
@QuarkusTest
@TestProfile(RetentionTestProfile.class)
class LedgerRetentionJobIT { ... }

public class RetentionTestProfile implements QuarkusTestProfile {
    @Override public String getConfigProfile() { return "retention-test"; }
}
```

Or use `@TestTransaction` on the inserting test class to ensure rollback after each test method.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The standard advice for H2 in-memory databases is to use `DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1` — without realising this keeps the database alive across the entire test suite. The failure is intermittent (depends on test execution order) and only manifests when running the full suite. The fix requires knowing that Quarkus profile properties can override the datasource URL per test class.

**See also:** [GE-20260508-ce2285](../jvm/GE-20260508-ce2285.md) — UUID-suffix business keys to prevent H2 in-memory shared-state conflicts

**See also:** [GE-20260529-8e127e](../jvm/GE-20260529-8e127e.md) — @Transactional on @QuarkusTest commits data — use @TestTransaction for rollback isolation

**See also:** [GE-20260620-29b8fc](../jvm/GE-20260620-29b8fc.md) — Quarkus eagerly starts @QuarkusTestResource from @Disabled test classes — breaks other tests

entries.title: Hibernate 6 JPQL arithmetic on MIN/MAX aggregates in HAVING is silently accepted by H2 but rejected by PostgreSQL
entries.body:
Hibernate 6 JPQL arithmetic on MIN/MAX aggregates in HAVING is silently accepted by H2 but rejected by PostgreSQL

## Hibernate 6 JPQL arithmetic on MIN/MAX aggregates in HAVING is silently accepted by H2 but rejected by PostgreSQL

### Symptom

A JPQL query using arithmetic on `MIN()` / `MAX()` aggregate results in a `HAVING` clause
throws at runtime on PostgreSQL:

```
org.hibernate.query.SemanticException: Operand of - is of type 'java.lang.Object'
which is not a numeric type (it is not an instance of 'java.lang.Number',
'java.time.Temporal', or 'java.time.TemporalAmount')
```

The same query works silently on H2 — all H2-backed `@QuarkusTest` pass green, giving
false confidence. The failure only appears when the query executes against PostgreSQL.

### Context

Typical pattern in a health-check or audit job:

```jpql
SELECT e.subjectId, e.tenancyId, COUNT(e), MIN(e.sequenceNumber), MAX(e.sequenceNumber)
FROM LedgerEntry e
GROUP BY e.subjectId, e.tenancyId
HAVING COUNT(e) != MAX(e.sequenceNumber) - MIN(e.sequenceNumber) + 1
```

`sequenceNumber` is declared as `int` (Java primitive). Hibernate 6's semantic checker
classifies `MIN(int field)` and `MAX(int field)` as `Object` rather than a numeric type
when they appear as arithmetic operands in a `HAVING` clause — but only when the
PostgreSQL dialect is active.

The query executes at job runtime (e.g. on a 1h scheduler interval), not at startup,
so the failure surfaces hours into production operation.

### Root cause

Hibernate 6 rewrote JPQL type resolution. Aggregate functions in `HAVING` arithmetic
are typed differently depending on the active dialect's type resolution path. The H2
dialect resolves `MIN`/`MAX` return types as numeric for arithmetic, while the
PostgreSQL dialect's path leaves them as `Object` — triggering the semantic checker.

This is a regression from Hibernate 5 where both dialects accepted the arithmetic.

### Fix

**Remove the arithmetic from HAVING. Filter in Java.**

Drop the `HAVING` clause entirely and filter in the result loop:

```jpql
-- Query (no HAVING):
SELECT e.subjectId, e.tenancyId, COUNT(e), MIN(e.sequenceNumber), MAX(e.sequenceNumber)
FROM LedgerEntry e
GROUP BY e.subjectId, e.tenancyId
```

```java
// Filter in Java — arithmetic is safe here:
for (SubjectSequenceStats s : repo.findSequenceStats()) {
    final long expected = (long) s.max() - s.min() + 1;
    if (s.count() == expected) continue;
    // anomaly detected
}
```

**Also: convert to `@NamedQuery` for startup validation.**

The query that failed did so at runtime. Had it been a `@NamedQuery` on the entity class,
Hibernate would have thrown the `SemanticException` at startup — not after the first
scheduler firing. Converting inline `em.createQuery()` calls to `@NamedQuery` turns a
1-hour-delayed runtime failure into an immediate boot failure:

```java
@NamedQuery(
    name = "LedgerEntry.findSequenceStats",
    query = """
        SELECT NEW com.example.SubjectSequenceStats(
            e.subjectId, e.tenancyId, COUNT(e), MIN(e.sequenceNumber), MAX(e.sequenceNumber)
        )
        FROM LedgerEntry e
        GROUP BY e.subjectId, e.tenancyId
        """)
@Entity
public abstract class LedgerEntry { ... }
```

The `NEW` constructor expression passes aggregate values as constructor arguments — no
arithmetic in the query — so the type checker does not fire.

### Why this is non-obvious

JPQL semantic analysis is supposed to be dialect-independent — it validates the JPQL
AST before any SQL is generated. The natural assumption is that if H2 tests pass, the
query is semantically valid. This assumption breaks here: H2's dialect contributes to
the type resolution path, and H2 resolves aggregate arithmetic types differently than
PostgreSQL does.

The failure mode is also severe: with a 1h scheduler interval and H2 tests giving a
false green, the bug can reach production undetected and remain invisible until the
first health-check execution.

### Why this fix

Removing HAVING arithmetic is preferable to using JPQL `CAST()` because:
1. It eliminates the type-resolution dependency entirely — no arithmetic means no type checking issue
2. The arithmetic is usually already computed in Java for display/logging purposes
3. It is universally portable across all Hibernate dialects

`CAST(MAX(e.sequenceNumber) AS Long)` would also work for the PostgreSQL dialect, but
it introduces a dialect-specific JPQL extension that may not be supported in all versions
of H2 or future dialects.

entries.title: CaseDefinition.hashCode() uses only namespace+name+version — content changes are invisible to hash-based drift detection
entries.body:
CaseDefinition.hashCode() uses only namespace+name+version — content changes are invisible to hash-based drift detection

### Symptom

Spec hash drift detection silently misses case definition content changes. Two `CaseDefinition` objects with identical namespace+name+version but completely different capabilities, workers, bindings, milestones, or goals produce the same `hashCode()` and are `equals()`.

### Context

Building a desired-state deployment module that tracks provisioned node specs via hash comparison. `CaseTypeNodeSpec` carried a `CaseDefinition definition` field. The record's auto-generated `hashCode()` delegates to `CaseDefinition.hashCode()` for that field — which only uses three identity fields.

### What was tried (didn't work)

Using `CaseDefinition` directly as a record field on `CaseTypeNodeSpec`, relying on Java record auto-generated `hashCode()` for spec hash drift detection. The assumption was that `CaseDefinition.hashCode()` would include all fields — it does not.

### Root cause

`CaseDefinition` explicitly overrides `hashCode()` and `equals()` to use only `namespace`, `name`, and `version`:

```java
public int hashCode() {
    return Objects.hash(new Object[]{this.namespace, this.name, this.version});
}

public boolean equals(Object o) {
    if (!(o instanceof CaseDefinition that)) return false;
    return Objects.equals(this.namespace, that.namespace)
        && Objects.equals(this.name, that.name)
        && Objects.equals(this.version, that.version);
}
```

This is a deliberate identity-based equality (two definitions with the same identity are "the same definition" regardless of content). But it defeats any system that uses `hashCode()` to detect content changes.

Additionally, `CaseDefinition` is a mutable JavaBean with setters (`setTitle()`, `setSummary()`) and mutable `ArrayList` fields (`getCapabilities().add(...)`). Placing it on an immutable record violates the value-type contract — external code can mutate the definition after record construction.

### Fix

Replace `CaseDefinition definition` with `Map<String, Object> definitionPayload` — the raw parsed YAML content:

```java
public record CaseTypeNodeSpec(
    String namespace, String name, String version,
    String title, String summary,
    String definitionFile,
    Map<String, Object> definitionPayload  // not CaseDefinition
) implements DeploymentNodeSpec { ... }
```

- Immutable via `Collections.unmodifiableMap(new LinkedHashMap<>(payload))`
- Correct recursive `hashCode()`/`equals()` for nested maps and lists
- Decoupled from engine-api's mutable domain class
- The handler constructs `CaseDefinition` from the payload at provision time

Use `Collections.unmodifiableMap()` instead of `Map.copyOf()` because YAML-parsed maps may contain null values.

### Why this is non-obvious

Java record auto-generated `hashCode()` delegates to each field's `hashCode()`. The assumption that `CaseDefinition.hashCode()` includes all fields is natural — you have to read the engine-api bytecode to discover the override. The class has 15+ fields; nothing in the API suggests only 3 are used for equality.

### Why this fix

Using `Map<String, Object>` instead of a typed domain object feels like a loss of type safety. But the alternative — fixing `CaseDefinition.hashCode()` to include all fields — would break every `HashMap<CaseDefinition, ...>` and `Set<CaseDefinition>` in the engine that relies on identity-based equality. The `Map<String, Object>` approach is the right boundary: the spec carries data, the handler interprets it.

### Alternatives considered

- **Fix CaseDefinition.hashCode()** — breaks all identity-based collections in the engine; the override is deliberate
- **Add a contentHash() method to CaseDefinition** — pollutes the domain class with drift-detection concerns
- **Wrap in a ContentHashable<CaseDefinition>** — unnecessary abstraction for a single use case

entries.title: EntityManager.merge() return value must be captured — the original instance stays detached
Mem0 /search scores are not comparable across separate calls
entries.body:
Mem0 /search scores are not comparable across separate calls

## Symptom

Multi-entity semantic search: fan out across N entities with separate `POST /search`
calls, collect results with their scores, sort merged list by score descending.
Entity A returns results scoring 0.4–0.9. Entity B returns results scoring 0.2–0.9.
The merged sort looks plausible but produces silently wrong relevance ordering.

## Context

Building a multi-entity `MemoryOrder.RELEVANCE` query fan-out. Each entity's search
is a separate POST /search call. The plan was to interleave results across entities by
their `score` fields to produce a globally relevance-ranked list.

## Root cause

The `score_and_rank()` function in `mem0/utils/scoring.py` normalises by `max_possible`:

```python
max_possible = 1.0
if has_bm25:   max_possible += 1.0          # → 2.0
if has_entity: max_possible += 0.5          # → 2.5
combined = min(raw_combined / max_possible, 1.0)
```

`max_possible` is **call-specific** — it depends on whether BM25 results exist for
that entity's memories. Entity A with many text-matched memories may normalise by 2.0;
entity B with no BM25 matches normalises by 1.0. A score of 0.9 in call A and 0.9
in call B do not represent the same underlying relevance — the denominators differ.

Additionally, each call applies its own threshold gate before returning results, so
the result sets are already pre-filtered independently.

## Fix (workaround)

Do not sort merged multi-entity results by score across calls. Instead:
- Preserve per-entity score ordering (each entity's results are internally valid)
- Concatenate in entity fan-out order (entity-order concat, not global score sort)
- Document this as a known limitation: multi-entity RELEVANCE is approximate

The only way to get globally comparable scores is to issue a single POST /search
call without filtering by individual `user_id` (search across all entities at once),
but this breaks entity isolation guarantees.

## Why this is non-obvious

The API returns a `score: float` field. The field name implies it is a globally
comparable similarity score (cosine similarity lives in [-1, 1] and IS comparable
across calls). The BM25 normalisation and per-call `max_possible` variability are
implementation details not exposed in any API documentation — discoverable only via
`mem0/utils/scoring.py`. Developers building multi-entity fans naturally sort by score
and produce subtly wrong results with no error.

entries.title: Disable squash/rebase merges across all org repos in one API loop
entries.body:
Disable squash/rebase merges across all org repos in one API loop

## Context

A GitHub organisation has accumulated repos where squash merges are enabled,
causing history loss. You want to enforce a consistent merge policy (no squash,
rebase allowed) across all repos at once.

## Technique

Use `gh api --method PATCH` in a loop over all repos:

```bash
for repo in engine ledger work qhorus claudony parent; do
  gh api --method PATCH repos/myorg/$repo \
    -f allow_squash_merge=false \
    -f allow_rebase_merge=true \
    -f allow_merge_commit=true \
    --jq '{repo:.full_name, squash:.allow_squash_merge, rebase:.allow_rebase_merge}'
done
```

The `--jq` flag confirms the new settings in the output without a separate
verification step. To also enable auto-delete of merged branches:
```bash
-f delete_branch_on_merge=true
```

## Why this is non-obvious

GitHub's UI requires navigating to each repo's Settings → General → Pull Requests
section individually. There is no bulk org-level policy enforcement in the UI for
merge strategy. The API approach takes seconds for any number of repos and produces
a verifiable output row per repo.

**See also:** GE-20260426-61854e — create GitHub org team and grant write access via gh CLI

entries.title: Stale qhorus local snapshot causes TABLE NOT FOUND in consuming project @QuarkusTest — schema-generation-strategy mismatch hides root cause
entries.body:
Stale qhorus local snapshot causes TABLE NOT FOUND in consuming project @QuarkusTest — schema-generation-strategy mismatch hides root cause

### Symptom
`@QuarkusTest` in the consuming project (casehub-aml) fails with:
```
SQLGrammarException: Could not prepare statement [Table "MESSAGE_LEDGER_ENTRY" not found]
```
The error points at a Flyway migration or entity-table mismatch. Flyway ran successfully; the entity class is correct. The consuming project's own code hasn't changed.

### Context
Two Maven projects with a producer/consumer relationship:
- **Producer** (casehub-qhorus): tests run with `hibernate-orm.database.generation=drop-and-create`. Flyway disabled for the qhorus datasource in tests. Hibernate generates the schema directly from entity class annotations.
- **Consumer** (casehub-aml): tests run with `hibernate-orm.database.generation=none`. Flyway IS enabled. Schema must match what Flyway migrations create.

A recent commit in the producer renamed the join table in the entity annotation (`@Table(name = "message_ledger_entry")`) but the corresponding Flyway migration SQL was also updated — in the source tree. The key issue: the locally-installed SNAPSHOT jar (`~/.m2`) still contains the **old migration SQL** (`V2000__*.sql` that creates `agent_message_ledger_entry`).

### Root cause
Producer tests use `drop-and-create`: Hibernate generates the schema from entity annotations — the new `message_ledger_entry` table is created automatically. Producer tests pass. The Flyway SQL inside the installed SNAPSHOT jar is stale, but it doesn't matter because producer tests don't run Flyway.

Consumer tests use `generation=none`: they depend entirely on Flyway to create the schema. Flyway runs the stale SQL from the installed jar, creating `agent_message_ledger_entry`. The entity annotation says `message_ledger_entry`. At runtime, Hibernate tries to query a table that doesn't exist.

`mvn compile` in the consumer succeeds because it uses cached class files from the producer (incremental compilation). No compile error surfaces. The failure only appears at `@QuarkusTest` startup.

### What was tried (didn't work)
1. Clean compile of the consumer project → still failed (cached class in consumer, stale jar from producer)
2. Checking Flyway migration files in the consumer → no issues found (consumer had no custom V2000)
3. Checking the entity annotation in the producer source → annotation was correct in source, matched the schema expectation

### Fix
Reinstall the producer SNAPSHOT from its own pom.xml before running consumer tests:

```bash
mvn --batch-mode install -DskipTests -f /path/to/producer/pom.xml
```

This recompiles the producer and installs a fresh jar to `~/.m2`, including the updated Flyway SQL. Consumer tests then pick up the correct migration.

### Why this is non-obvious
The failure looks like a Flyway configuration bug or a schema mismatch in the consumer project. The producer tests pass (they use `drop-and-create`, bypassing Flyway entirely). There is no compile error. The version number of the SNAPSHOT jar doesn't change. Nothing in the consumer's own code changed. The only diagnostic clue is the table name discrepancy between the error (`MESSAGE_LEDGER_ENTRY`) and the entity annotation (`message_ledger_entry`), but even that points to a consumer misconfiguration, not a stale jar from a different repo.

The fundamental issue is that the two database generation strategies (producer: `drop-and-create`, consumer: `generation=none + Flyway`) create an inconsistency that is invisible to Maven's incremental build and only surfaces at runtime in the consumer.

### See also
- GE-20260422-7ace3d — stale Maven jar causes NoSuchMethodError (different symptom, same underlying cause)
- GE-20260424-275fdc — stale SNAPSHOT annotations cause CDI errors

entries.title: casehub-qhorus MessageService methods using Panache static calls bypass InMemory store in @QuarkusTest
@Alternative on a CDI bean silently suppresses all @DefaultBean beans of the same type — they vanish from the container entirely
@ConsumeEvent(blocking=true) and @Transactional on the same method work correctly in Quarkus
@DefaultBean silently suppressed by any Instance<T> peer — breaks multi-implementation SPI patterns
entries.body:
@DefaultBean silently suppressed by any Instance<T> peer — breaks multi-implementation SPI patterns

### Symptom
Agent drift checking silently stops working after adding `@DefaultBean` to `AgentDriftChecker` — no error, no warning. The bean is never injected.

### Context
`DeploymentActualStateAdapter` injects `Instance<NodeDriftChecker>` and builds a `Map<String, NodeDriftChecker>` keyed by `nodeType()`. Four implementations exist: `AgentDriftChecker`, `ChannelDriftChecker`, `CaseTypeDriftChecker`, `TrustPolicyDriftChecker`. Each serves a different `nodeType()` but all implement `NodeDriftChecker`.

The goal was to make `AgentDriftChecker` replaceable by a richer implementation via classpath presence — the standard `@DefaultBean` → `@Alternative @Priority(1)` pattern used successfully elsewhere (e.g. `NoOpAgentStateStore` → `InMemoryAgentStateStore`).

### Root cause
Quarkus `@DefaultBean` suppression operates on **bean types** (interface types), not concrete classes. When `AgentDriftChecker` is `@DefaultBean` and `ChannelDriftChecker` (also implementing `NodeDriftChecker`) is `@ApplicationScoped`, ArC sees a non-default bean with the same interface type and suppresses the `@DefaultBean` — regardless of the fact that they serve completely different runtime roles via `nodeType()`.

The `@DefaultBean` pattern is designed for **single-injection-point replacement**: one interface, one default, one override. It is incompatible with `Instance<T>` multi-bean collection where implementations are distinguished by a runtime discriminator.

### What was tried (didn't work)
1. `@DefaultBean` on `AgentDriftChecker` — suppressed by `ChannelDriftChecker` existing
2. `@Alternative @Priority(1)` on the replacement — both beans appear in `Instance<T>` iteration, `HashMap.put()` non-deterministic winner for the same `nodeType()` key

### Fix
Do not use `@DefaultBean` or `@Alternative` for multi-implementation SPI patterns collected via `Instance<T>`. Instead, either:
- Enrich the existing bean directly (no displacement needed)
- Add priority-aware resolution in the adapter: `checkers.merge(nodeType, checker, (existing, incoming) -> getPriority(incoming) > getPriority(existing) ? incoming : existing)`

### Why this is non-obvious
The `@DefaultBean` pattern works flawlessly for single-injection patterns throughout the codebase (6+ SPIs use it). The mental model — "default vanishes when a better one arrives" — is correct for that context. The failure mode here is that *any* `NodeDriftChecker` implementation (not just an agent-specific one) triggers suppression. No error, no warning, no log. The bean simply disappears from the `Instance<T>` iteration.

### Why this fix
Enriching the existing bean avoids all CDI displacement complexity. The comparison logic (pure Java utility) lives in the type-owning module; the SPI implementation stays in the consumer module unchanged. No CDI annotations change, no new beans, no Instance<T> interaction to reason about.

### Alternatives considered
- Priority-aware adapter resolution — works but adds complexity to the adapter for a problem that only exists because of the displacement pattern
- Bridge module with `@Alternative @Priority(1)` — both beans appear in Instance iteration; HashMap collision is non-deterministic
- Composition approach (AgentNodeSpec wrapping AgentDescriptor) — blocked by tenancyId being required in AgentDescriptor's compact constructor

**See also:** GE-20260623-c651a1 — removing @Alternative from one bean in a multi-implementation chain silently changes resolution, another CDI displacement gotcha.

entries.title: Use @QuarkusTestProfile.getConfigOverrides() to isolate per-class @QuarkusTest config
UUID-suffix business keys in @QuarkusTest to prevent H2 in-memory shared-state conflicts
entries.body:
UUID-suffix business keys in @QuarkusTest to prevent H2 in-memory shared-state conflicts

## Problem

`@QuarkusTest` with `jdbc:h2:mem:dbname;DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1` shares a single in-memory H2 database across all test classes in the same JVM run. `@TestTransaction` rolls back — but only for direct persistence calls. REST Assured HTTP calls commit in their own transactions and are not rolled back. Hardcoded business keys (e.g. `protocolId = "ONCOL-001"`) accumulate across test classes and fail on unique constraints when tests run in sequence, producing non-deterministic failures that depend on test ordering.

## Technique

Suffix every test-data business key with `UUID.randomUUID()`:

```java
// Instead of:
.body("{\"protocolId\": \"ONCOL-001\", ...}")

// Use:
.body("{\"protocolId\": \"ONCOL-" + UUID.randomUUID() + "\", ...}")
```

Or in a helper:

```java
private String uniqueProtocol() {
    return "ONCOL-" + UUID.randomUUID();
}
```

This costs nothing, eliminates state conflicts entirely, and requires no `@TestTransaction` (which breaks REST Assured assertions anyway — see related GE-0036).

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

`@TestTransaction` is the documented Quarkus mechanism for test isolation. Developers apply it expecting it to roll back all state, then find REST Assured tests still failing due to stale data. The disconnect — that HTTP calls commit outside `@TestTransaction`'s scope — is not surfaced in the Quarkus testing docs. The UUID-suffix pattern is simple once known but easy to miss when you're debugging apparently-random test ordering failures.

## Constraints

Applies when: tests use `DB_CLOSE_DELAY=-1` (shared H2), data is inserted via HTTP endpoints (not direct EntityManager), and there is a unique constraint on the business key column.

**See also:** [GE-20260420-d99177](../quarkus/GE-20260420-d99177.md) — @QuarkusTest classes sharing same H2 URL contaminate each other's data

**See also:** [GE-20260607-4bb9a7](GE-20260607-4bb9a7.md) — await on specific caseId state, not shared static list, to prevent cross-test contamination

**See also:** [GE-20260610-952c8d](GE-20260610-952c8d.md) — two-assertion write-invariant test for child-entity tenant inheritance in multi-tenant systems

entries.title: ChatModel.doChat(ChatRequest) is the override point for test doubles — not chat(ChatRequest)
Canonical CloudEvent adapter pattern — 7 rules for CDI async adapters
entries.body:
With `.whenComplete()`: exception preserved on the stage → `thenCompose` skips its lambda
→ exception surfaces to SmallRye → message nacked → retried or dead-lettered.

Rule 3 applies **only in pure fire-and-forget dispatch** (the `CompletionStage` is not
returned or chained further).

CDI `@ObservesAsync` adapters are always fire-and-forget.

SmallRye `@Incoming` consumers return the stage for ack/nack signalling — they are not.

### Alternatives considered

- **Let exceptions propagate from `@ObservesAsync`** — rejected; CDI swallows them silently on the managed executor thread
- **Use `@Observes` (sync) instead of `@ObservesAsync`** — rejected; would block the original event producer's thread
- **Static ObjectMapper** — rejected; isolates serialisation config, breaks when app registers custom serializers
- **SEVERE log level for failures** — rejected; serialisation failure of a single event is degraded, not catastrophic; SEVERE causes alert fatigue

**See also:** [GE-20260423-daef97](GE-20260423-daef97.md) — CDI event.fire() does not deliver to @ObservesAsync — fireAsync() required (rule 1 of the canonical adapter pattern) | [GE-20260531-e1ce47](GE-20260531-e1ce47.md) — CDI @Observes and @ObservesAsync are separate delivery channels | [GE-20260428-f075ef](GE-20260428-f075ef.md) — Race-free CompletableFuture per-item pattern for CDI async event tests

entries.title: @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
@Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

## @Alternative @Priority(n) in CDI 4.0/Quarkus globally activates the alternative — causes AmbiguousResolutionException

### Stack
Quarkus 3.31.1, CDI 4.0

### Symptom
Multiple beans implement the same SPI interface. Each alternative is annotated with
`@Alternative @Priority(10)` as well as `@ApplicationScoped`. On Quarkus startup, the
application fails with `AmbiguousResolutionException` — all implementations are active
simultaneously.

```
AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type ContextDiffStrategy
and qualifiers [@Default]: [TopLevelContextDiffStrategy, JsonPatchContextDiffStrategy, 
NoOpContextDiffStrategy]
```

### Context
SPI pattern where one default bean and two `@Alternative` beans implement an interface.
The intent was to activate one alternative at a time via
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`.

### What was tried
Adding `@Priority(10)` alongside `@Alternative` — expected this to be a ranking hint
that would help CDI resolve ambiguity. Instead, `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is what
globally activates the bean in CDI 4.0, making all three implementations simultaneously
active and causing the ambiguous resolution error.

### Root cause
In CDI 4.0 (Jakarta EE 10), `@Alternative @Priority(n)` is a shorthand for globally
activating an alternative across the entire application — no `beans.xml` or
`selected-alternatives` config needed. When ALL alternatives carry this annotation,
they are ALL active, which breaks injection points expecting a single bean.

### Solution 1 — Remove @Priority, use selected-alternatives config
**Approach:** Strip `@Priority` from all alternatives; activate one via config.
**Pros:** Per-profile/per-module control without code changes; the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching.
**Cons:** Requires a config entry per activation; all alternatives default to inactive.

Remove `@Priority` from all `@Alternative` beans. Activate a specific alternative only
via `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`:

```java
// WRONG — @Priority globally activates the alternative in CDI 4.0
@Alternative
@Priority(10)
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }

// CORRECT — @Alternative only; activation controlled by config
@Alternative
@ApplicationScoped
public class MyAlternativeImpl implements MyInterface { ... }
```

```properties
# application.properties — activate the desired alternative
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=com.example.MyAlternativeImpl
```

### Solution 2 — Use different @Priority values to establish precedence
**Approach:** Keep `@Alternative @Priority` but assign distinct priority values; CDI resolves to the highest.
**Pros:** No config entry needed; the default (lowest priority) is always active, the override (highest priority) wins when present on the classpath. Works naturally for optional-module patterns where a higher-capability implementation should auto-replace the baseline.
**Cons:** All alternatives are always globally active (the core CDI 4.0 behaviour). The "default" must have the lowest priority; adding a third implementation requires careful priority assignment.

```java
// Default — always active; lowest priority loses when a higher one is present
@Alternative
@Priority(0)
@ApplicationScoped
public class ClaimFirstStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }

// Override — higher priority wins; active only when on the classpath
@Alternative
@Priority(1)
@ApplicationScoped
public class SemanticWorkerSelectionStrategy implements WorkerSelectionStrategy { ... }
```

CDI injects the `@Priority(1)` bean when both are present; falls back to `@Priority(0)` when only
the default module is on the classpath. No `beans.xml` or `selected-alternatives` required.

**When to choose Solution 2 over Solution 1:** When the override is an optional module (not always
on the classpath) and the default must always be present. Config-based activation (Solution 1)
requires an explicit entry even for the default case; priority-based resolution (Solution 2) makes
the default zero-configuration.

### Why this is non-obvious
The `@Priority` annotation is commonly used alongside `@Alternative` in CDI tutorials
and examples from the CDI 2.x era, where `@Priority` on an alternative was a *ranking*
hint. In CDI 4.0 the semantics changed — `@Priority` on an `@Alternative` is now an
*activation* mechanism (equivalent to listing it in `beans.xml` with a priority). The
Quarkus docs for `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` don't warn about this interaction.

### Why this fix
`quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` gives per-profile/per-module control via config,
which is the intended Quarkus mechanism for SPI switching (same as how `PanningStrategyLoopControl`
and other engine SPIs are activated). It doesn't require code changes to switch backends.

**See also:** GE-20260422-ebb91d — AmbiguousResolutionException from multiple unqualified @ApplicationScoped beans

**See also:** GE-20260427-97650e "CDI ambiguity when adding second implementation of a quarkus-ledger repository interface"
**See also:** GE-20260427-62d3ab — use @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans in @QuarkusTest to spy on SPI call sites
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f — @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in a test-helper jar are invisible to downstream @QuarkusTest — no Jandex index](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md)

### Update — Test-refactoring trap: two classes declaring the same @Alternative

During a test refactoring, if two `@QuarkusTest` classes *simultaneously* declare an
`@Alternative @Priority(2)` inner class implementing the same type, Quarkus boot fails
with `AmbiguousResolutionException` even though `mvn test-compile` passes cleanly. The
compile succeeds because both classes are syntactically valid; the CDI conflict only
surfaces at application startup.

entries.title: Removing @Alternative from one bean in a multi-implementation CDI chain silently changes resolution — no AmbiguousResolutionException
entries.body:
Removing @Alternative from one bean in a multi-implementation CDI chain silently changes resolution — no AmbiguousResolutionException

### Symptom

After removing `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from one CDI bean, a different bean
silently becomes the active implementation. No `AmbiguousResolutionException`, no
warning, no log. Tests that depend on the removed bean's behaviour fail with
unrelated-looking assertion errors.

### Context

Three implementations of the same port interface coexist in the build:

| Bean | Annotations | Role |
|------|------------|------|
| `PrReviewService` | `@ApplicationScoped @DefaultBean` | Layer 1 naive baseline |
| `QhorusPrReviewService` | `@ApplicationScoped @Alternative @Priority(1)` | Layer 3 qhorus messaging |
| `PrReviewCaseService` | `@ApplicationScoped @Alternative @Priority(2)` | Layer 5 engine (active) |

This is the CDI displacement pattern for progressive layer integration — each layer
adds an implementation; CDI priority determines which is active at runtime.

### What was tried (didn't work)

Removed `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from `PrReviewCaseService` to fix an anti-pattern
documented in ARC42STORIES §8 ("CDI priority gymnastics"). Expected: the plain
`@ApplicationScoped` bean would still displace the `@DefaultBean` baseline via
Quarkus's `@DefaultBean` semantics.

### Root cause

CDI resolution rules interact in a non-obvious way:

1. `@DefaultBean` beans are suppressed when any non-`@DefaultBean` bean exists ✅
2. Activated `@Alternative` beans (with `@Priority`) take precedence over regular beans ⚠️
3. After removing `@Alternative` from `PrReviewCaseService`, it becomes a regular bean
4. `QhorusPrReviewService` (`@Alternative @Priority(1)`) is still an activated alternative
5. **Rule 2 applies**: the activated alternative wins over the regular bean
6. `QhorusPrReviewService` becomes the active implementation — silently

The mental model "I'm just removing unnecessary annotations" is wrong. The annotations
are load-bearing: they participate in a priority chain where every bean's annotation
set matters.

### Fix

All implementations in the `@Alternative` chain must be addressed together, not one at
a time. To properly fix the anti-pattern:

- Either make ALL non-baseline implementations use `@DefaultBean` displacement
  (remove `@Alternative @Priority` from all of them and ensure only one non-`@DefaultBean` exists)
- Or keep the `@Alternative @Priority` chain intact and accept it as the mechanism
  for layer coexistence

Do not remove `@Alternative` from one bean without checking what other `@Alternative`
implementations exist for the same interface.

### Why this is non-obvious

Most developers reason about CDI alternatives in pairs: "my bean vs the default."
The three-way interaction (DefaultBean + Alternative(1) + Alternative(2)) is rarely
encountered, and the silent resolution change (no exception, wrong bean) gives no
diagnostic signal. The symptom (test assertion failure on unrelated data) is
completely disconnected from the cause (CDI annotation change).

### Why this fix

The obvious approach — "just remove the anti-pattern annotation" — is wrong because
it treats the annotations as decoration rather than as a load-bearing priority chain.
The fix requires understanding the full set of implementations and their annotation
interactions before changing any single one.

### Alternatives considered

- Remove `@Alternative @Priority(2)` from only the active bean → silently breaks resolution (this gotcha)
- Remove `@Alternative` from ALL implementations and use `@DefaultBean` displacement → requires ensuring exactly one non-`@DefaultBean` impl, and retiring or `@DefaultBean`-ing the intermediate layers
- Keep `@Alternative @Priority` chain as-is → accepted as the working mechanism until a coordinated cleanup addresses all implementations together

**See also:** GE-20260626-c21b02 — @DefaultBean suppression by any Instance<T> peer, a different silent failure in the same CDI displacement space.

entries.title: @ApplicationScoped POJO + @Produces for same type causes CDI ambiguous bean resolution
CDI AmbiguousResolutionException when multiple @ApplicationScoped beans implement the same SPI interface
entries.body:
CDI AmbiguousResolutionException when multiple @ApplicationScoped beans implement the same SPI interface

## Symptom

`@QuarkusTest` fails at boot with `AmbiguousResolutionException: Ambiguous dependencies for type X`. The production code compiles and the beans look correct.

## Context

Defining multiple `@ApplicationScoped` implementations of the same SPI interface (e.g. three `SkillProfileProvider` implementations) so users can choose which to activate. All three are unqualified, so CDI cannot resolve which one to inject at a single injection point.

## What was tried

Adding all three as `@ApplicationScoped` beans, assuming CDI would pick the first one or throw a clear build-time error.

## Root cause

CDI requires exactly one unambiguous bean per injection type when no qualifier is used. Multiple unqualified `@ApplicationScoped` beans implementing the same interface all compete — CDI cannot resolve the injection point and throws at container startup, not at compile time.

## Fix

Designate exactly one implementation as the default (leave it unqualified `@ApplicationScoped`). Mark all others with `@Alternative`:

```java
// Default — CDI injects this when no override is present
@ApplicationScoped
public class WorkerProfileSkillProfileProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }

// Not active by default — user activates via @Alternative @Priority(1)
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
public class CapabilitiesSkillProfileProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }

@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
public class ResolutionHistorySkillProfileProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }
```

Users override the default at their own application level:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
@Alternative
@Priority(1)
public class MyCustomProvider implements SkillProfileProvider { ... }
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The error only appears at `@QuarkusTest` boot — compile succeeds silently. The fix (marking non-default implementations `@Alternative`) is easy once known, but the CDI resolution rules around `@Alternative` vs unqualified beans are non-obvious. The natural instinct is to let the user pick via config, not via CDI qualifiers.

**See also:** GE-20260415-884e48 — @Alternative @Priority(n) globally activates the alternative in CDI 4.0 | GE-20260417-c59817 — quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives activates @Alternative beans in application.properties | GE-20260421-1cfae6 — @Produces @DefaultBean provides consumer-replaceable CDI defaults without @Alternative

**See also:** [GE-20260428-9311f8](GE-20260428-9311f8.md) — casehub-engine @ApplicationScoped no-op SPI beans collide with consumer implementations when engine is indexed | [GE-20260427-97650e](GE-20260427-97650e.md) — CDI ambiguity when adding second implementation of a quarkus-ledger repository interface

**See also:** GE-20260427-62d3ab "Use @Alternative @Priority(1) inner beans in @QuarkusTest to spy on SPI call sites without Mockito"
**See also:** GE-20260427-c2b84f — @Alternative @Priority(1) beans in test-helper jar are invisible without Jandex index

## Update — Variant: two @DefaultBean impls for the same type

`@DefaultBean` in Quarkus Arc means "use this if no non-default bean for this type exists."
When **two** `@DefaultBean @ApplicationScoped` beans implement the same interface, Arc cannot
resolve the injection point — both are default, neither wins — and throws
`AmbiguousResolutionException` as soon as any production bean injects that type. The symptom
only surfaces once an `@Inject` for the interface is added; before that the ambiguity exists
silently in the CDI container.

**Scenario:** Two library modules each provide a `@DefaultBean CurrentPrincipal` for
"uncontested" environments (`MockCurrentPrincipal` from casehub-platform, `QhorusInboundCurrentPrincipal`
from casehub-qhorus). Adding `@Inject CurrentPrincipal` to any production bean in the app
module triggers the error — the injection point forces CDI to resolve and it finds two equals.

**Fix — exclude one at application level:**

```properties
# application.properties — exclude the stub; the runtime principal wins
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=io.casehub.platform.mock.MockCurrentPrincipal
```

**Test fix — use quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives to force a third, definitive impl:**

```properties
# src/test/resources/application.properties
quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives=io.casehub.platform.testing.FixedCurrentPrincipal
```

**Why @DefaultBean differs from @Alternative:** `@DefaultBean` is not in the CDI spec — it is
a Quarkus Arc extension that says "this bean loses to any explicit non-default bean." But when
two `@DefaultBean` beans compete, there is no winner — Arc throws rather than picking arbitrarily.
The `@Alternative` pattern (one winner, rest marked alternative) is the correct SPI design for
exactly-one-at-a-time. `@DefaultBean` is for extension-provided stubs that yield to consumer
overrides, not for multiple peers. Two `@DefaultBean` peers should be refactored so that only one
module provides the default; the other moves to `@Alternative`.

entries.title: @Readiness annotation is both a MicroProfile Health classifier and a CDI qualifier — @Inject HealthCheck fails without the qualifier
entries.body:
@Readiness annotation is both a MicroProfile Health classifier and a CDI qualifier — @Inject HealthCheck fails without the qualifier

### Symptom

`@Inject AgentHealthCheck healthCheck` in a `@QuarkusTest` throws `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` — the bean exists and is registered via `HealthBuildItem`, but CDI reports no matching bean for the injection point.

### Context

Writing a Quarkus extension that registers a `@Readiness HealthCheck` bean via `HealthBuildItem`. The bean class has `@Readiness` on the class declaration. A test tries to inject it directly to call `healthCheck.call()` and assert on the response.

### What was tried (didn't work)

```java
@Inject
AgentHealthCheck healthCheck;
```

CDI fails at deployment time with `UnsatisfiedResolutionException` because the injection point has the implicit `@Default` qualifier, but the bean has `@Readiness` as its qualifier.

### Root cause

`@Readiness` is annotated with `@Qualifier` in the MicroProfile Health API. This means it serves double duty: it classifies the health check as a readiness probe for the health subsystem, AND it acts as a CDI qualifier that restricts how the bean can be injected. Plain `@Inject` without `@Readiness` on the injection point will not match.

### Fix

Use `Arc.container().select()` with the `Readiness.Literal.INSTANCE` qualifier:

```java
AgentHealthCheck healthCheck = (AgentHealthCheck) Arc.container()
        .select(HealthCheck.class, Readiness.Literal.INSTANCE).get();
HealthCheckResponse response = healthCheck.call();
```

Or add the qualifier to the injection point:

```java
@Inject
@Readiness
HealthCheck healthCheck;
```

### Why this is non-obvious

`@Readiness` reads as a health check *type classifier* ("this is a readiness check, not a liveness check"), not as a CDI *qualifier* that restricts injection resolution. The dual role is not mentioned prominently in MicroProfile Health docs. Other extensions in the same codebase (e.g. MCP's `McpClientHealthCheck`) use `@Readiness` but never inject the bean directly in tests, so the qualifier effect is invisible until someone tries direct `@Inject`.

**See also:** [GE-20260604-d08c9f](GE-20260604-d08c9f.md) — @Transactional and @Tool can coexist on the same method (related annotation-on-method interaction)

**See also:** [GE-20260615-6c4767](../casehub-engine/GE-20260615-6c4767.md) — adding custom CDI qualifier to @ApplicationScoped removes @Default

Quarkus CDI does not scan @ApplicationScoped beans in plain JAR module dependencies
entries.body:
Quarkus CDI does not scan @ApplicationScoped beans in plain JAR module dependencies

## Behaviour

In a Quarkus multi-module Maven project, if a plain library module (no Quarkus Maven plugin, `packaging=jar`) contains `@ApplicationScoped` CDI beans and is added as a `<dependency>` of the Quarkus application module, those beans are NOT discovered by CDI. Injection of these beans at runtime fails with "Unsatisfied dependency for type X".

## Context

Occurs when splitting a single-module Quarkus app into a library module + Quarkus app module. The library beans compile fine and the JAR is on the classpath, but Quarkus CDI ignores them.

## Root Cause

Quarkus CDI bean discovery only indexes classes in JARs that are either: (a) the application itself, (b) have a Jandex index (`META-INF/jandex.idx`), or (c) are explicitly listed in `quarkus.index-dependency`. Plain JARs without Jandex are not scanned.

## Fix

Add to `claudony-app/src/main/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.group-id=com.example
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.artifact-id=mylib-core
```

Or add the Jandex Maven plugin to the library module's `pom.xml`:

```xml
<plugin>
    <groupId>io.smallrye</groupId>
    <artifactId>jandex-maven-plugin</artifactId>
    <version>3.1.2</version>
    <executions>
        <execution>
            <goals><goal>jandex</goal></goals>
        </execution>
    </executions>
</plugin>
```

## Why Undocumented

The Quarkus docs for multi-module projects mention the Jandex plugin as an option but do not clearly state that without it (or `index-dependency`), CDI beans in library JARs are silently ignored. The error message "Unsatisfied dependency" doesn't hint at the indexing problem — it looks like the bean simply doesn't exist.

**See also:** [GE-20260421-03fa72](GE-20260421-03fa72.md) — Jandex Maven plugin approach to same problem; [GE-20260423-a01832](GE-20260423-a01832.md) — @Alternative test JARs same root cause; [GE-0133](GE-0133.md) — CDI silently ignores @ApplicationScoped beans in third-party JARs without Jandex
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md) — same CDI scanning root cause for @Alternative beans in test-helper JARs

**See also:** GE-20260428-9311f8 — indexed beans cause CDI collision (opposite problem)

entries.title: Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422
entries.body:
Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422

## Symptom

Code compiles with a `Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` reference in a `WebApplicationException` or `Response.status()` call, but the IDE shows an error and the build fails:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
  location: class Response.Status
```

## Root cause

`Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` (HTTP 422) was added in JAX-RS 3.1 / Jakarta EE 10. Projects targeting Jakarta EE 9 / JAX-RS 3.0 (which Quarkus 3.x may use depending on the BOM) don't have this constant.

## Fix

Use the raw integer `422` directly:

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

entries.title: Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()
entries.body:
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()

## What Happened

Code used `Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` to offload blocking work onto a worker pool in a Mutiny pipeline. The method name is logical (Mutiny has `runSubscriptionOn` for blocking work) but the method does not exist. The compile error is clear but the fix is non-obvious because no similar `getDefaultBlockingExecutor` pattern appears in Mutiny documentation.

## Why This Is Non-obvious

The Mutiny `Infrastructure` class has `getDefaultExecutor()` — the default worker pool suitable for blocking work when combined with `runSubscriptionOn`. There is no separately named "blocking" executor. The distinction between the IO thread and the worker pool is handled by `runSubscriptionOn` itself, not by a separate executor variant. The name "blocking" is a common mental model but doesn't map to any Mutiny API.

## Fix

```java
// Wrong — does not exist:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor())

// Correct:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultExecutor())
```

## Invalidation Triggers

Revisit if Mutiny adds a named `getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` alias in a future release.

**See also:** [GE-20260602-c38360](GE-20260602-c38360.md) — PanacheQuery.stream() does not exist in Quarkus Hibernate Reactive Panache

**See also:** [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist

**See also:** [GE-20260621-cb4492](GE-20260621-cb4492.md) — drools-engine artifact does not exist at Drools 10.1.0 | [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist (same class of API-that-doesn't-exist gotcha)

U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it
entries.body:
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark) is a BiDi control character but sits outside the C0/C1 ranges — standard control-char filters miss it

### What happened
A field validator banning BiDi control characters to prevent prompt injection implemented a standard check: reject codepoints 0–31 (C0), 127–159 (C1), U+200E/200F (LRM/RLM), U+202A–202E (LRE/RLE/PDF/LRO/RLO), and U+2066–2069 (LRI/RLI/FSI/PDI). Code review caught that U+061C was missing. The validator would have passed strings containing the Arabic Letter Mark.

### Root cause
U+061C (Arabic Letter Mark, ALM) was added in Unicode 6.3 as a BiDi control character. It is in the Arabic block (U+0600–U+06FF), not in any standard control character range (C0: 0x00–0x1F, C1: 0x80–0x9F). It has no visible glyph and controls BiDi text direction.

Standard documentation and implementations of "ban control characters" focus on C0/C1 ranges. The dedicated BiDi control ranges (LRM/RLM/LRE etc.) are more widely known. U+061C predates none of these — it was simply added later and sits in an unexpected block.

### Fix
Add explicit check for U+061C alongside other BiDi controls:

```java
private static boolean isBanned(final int cp) {
    if (cp <= 0x001F) return true;                      // C0 control chars
    if (cp >= 0x007F && cp <= 0x009F) return true;     // DEL + C1 control chars
    if (cp == 0x061C) return true;                      // ALM — Arabic Letter Mark (BiDi, Unicode 6.3)
    if (cp == 0x200E || cp == 0x200F) return true;     // LRM, RLM
    if (cp >= 0x202A && cp <= 0x202E) return true;     // LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO
    if (cp >= 0x2066 && cp <= 0x2069) return true;     // LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI
    if (cp == 0x200B || cp == 0xFEFF) return true;     // ZWSP, BOM/ZWNBSP
    if (cp == 0x200C || cp == 0x200D) return true;     // ZWNJ, ZWJ
    if (cp == 0x2028 || cp == 0x2029) return true;     // LINE SEP, PARA SEP
    return false;
}
```

### Why this is non-obvious
The Unicode BiDi control characters everyone knows (LRM, RLM, LRE, RLE, PDF, LRO, RLO, LRI, RLI, FSI, PDI) are all in the General Punctuation block (U+2000–U+206F). U+061C is in the Arabic block. No standard "control character" resource bundles it with the others. The Unicode BiDi Algorithm spec mentions it, but security-focused implementations typically enumerate the well-known ranges without reading the full BiDi spec for outliers.

entries.title: TIMESTAMPTZ not recognised by H2 in MODE=PostgreSQL — use TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE
entries.body:
TIMESTAMPTZ not recognised by H2 in MODE=PostgreSQL — use TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE

## Context

Writing Flyway migrations for a Quarkus module that uses PostgreSQL in production and H2
with `MODE=PostgreSQL` in `@QuarkusTest`. The migration uses `TIMESTAMPTZ` (PostgreSQL's
abbreviated type name for `TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE`).

## Symptom

Flyway migration fails during `@QuarkusTest` startup with a SQL syntax error even though
`MODE=PostgreSQL` is configured. Production (PostgreSQL) works fine.

## Root Cause

`TIMESTAMPTZ` is a PostgreSQL-specific abbreviation. H2 implements only a subset of
PostgreSQL syntax in `MODE=PostgreSQL` — it recognises the full form
`TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE` but not the abbreviated `TIMESTAMPTZ`.

## Fix

Use the full form in Flyway migrations:

```sql
-- Fails in H2 even with MODE=PostgreSQL
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,

-- Works in both PostgreSQL and H2 MODE=PostgreSQL
created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE NOT NULL,
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

PostgreSQL documentation and tooling widely use `TIMESTAMPTZ` — it appears in pg_dump
output, official PostgreSQL tutorials, and StackOverflow answers. `MODE=PostgreSQL` in H2
creates an expectation that PostgreSQL-idiomatic SQL will work. The error message points
to a SQL syntax failure, not to a type-name unsupported issue, making diagnosis indirect.

**See also:** [GE-20260603-86f2a9](GE-20260603-86f2a9.md) — H2 does not support INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING | [GE-20260605-0ffc19](GE-20260605-0ffc19.md) — H2 does not support SIMILAR TO in CHECK constraints

**See also:** [GE-20260617-03c1a3](GE-20260617-03c1a3.md) — H2 MODE=PostgreSQL ON CONFLICT syntax limitations

entries.title: static new ObjectMapper() silently fails to serialize Instant in Quarkus — use @Inject ObjectMapper
entries.body:
static new ObjectMapper() silently fails to serialize Instant in Quarkus — use @Inject ObjectMapper

## Symptom

An SSE endpoint (or any endpoint) that serialises `SessionResponse` objects silently returns `[]` (empty array) instead of the actual workers. No exception is visible to the caller — the exception is caught internally and the fallback value is returned. The server logs may show a `com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidDefinitionException` at DEBUG level.

## Context

`SessionResource` in Claudony had:
```java
private static final ObjectMapper MAPPER = new ObjectMapper();
```

`SessionResponse` contains `Instant createdAt` and `Instant lastActive`. Serializing a `List<SessionResponse>` through this `MAPPER` threw:
```
com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidDefinitionException:
Java 8 date/time type `java.time.Instant` not supported by default:
add Module "com.fasterxml.jackson.datatype:jackson-datatype-jsr310"
```

The exception was caught in the `buildCaseSnapshot` helper and the fallback `"data: []"` was returned silently.

## Root Cause

`new ObjectMapper()` creates a bare instance with no modules registered. Quarkus configures its CDI-managed `ObjectMapper` bean with `JavaTimeModule` (and other modules like `Jdk8Module`) automatically. A static field bypasses this configuration.

## Fix

Replace the static field with CDI injection:

```java
// WRONG — no JavaTimeModule
private static final ObjectMapper MAPPER = new ObjectMapper();

// CORRECT — uses Quarkus-configured instance with all modules
@Inject
ObjectMapper MAPPER;
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The class had a `static final` `ObjectMapper` that worked fine for other uses (simple POJOs without time fields). The failure only surfaced when the snapshot function started serialising `SessionResponse` with `Instant` fields. Because the exception was caught with a fallback, the endpoint returned valid JSON (`[]`) — not an error — making it very hard to notice.

GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing
entries.body:
GreenMailExtension.getGreenMail() is protected — inaccessible from test code without subclassing

## Behaviour

`GreenMailExtension` extends `GreenMailProxy`. In `greenmail-junit5 2.1.3`, `GreenMailProxy.getGreenMail()` is declared `protected`:

```bash
$ javap -classpath greenmail-junit5-2.1.3.jar com.icegreen.greenmail.junit5.GreenMailExtension
...
protected com.icegreen.greenmail.util.GreenMail getGreenMail();
```

Attempting to call it from test code:

```java
GreenMail mail = GREEN_MAIL.getGreenMail(); // compile error: getGreenMail() has protected access
```

## Why This Matters

`GreenMail` (the bare class) has a `deliver(MimeMessage)` method and other useful internal APIs. The extension does not expose them. Since `getGreenMail()` is protected, you cannot retrieve the underlying instance to call them.

**Workaround:** In `@QuarkusTestResource` implementations that construct `GreenMail` directly (not via the extension), you hold the instance yourself as a static field — giving you full access to `deliver()`, `getManagers()`, etc.

## Why It's Undocumented

The Greenmail documentation and README make no mention of this access restriction. The Greenmail JUnit 5 guide shows the extension as a drop-in for the JUnit 4 Rule and implies equivalent functionality. The only way to discover the `protected` modifier is via `javap` on the jar.

**See also:** [GE-20260529-aa8445](GE-20260529-aa8445.md) — GreenMailExtension has no public deliver() method
