quarkus-langchain4j-core Quarkus extension stalls @QuarkusTest augmentation when no model provider is configured
entries.body:
quarkus-langchain4j-core Quarkus extension stalls @QuarkusTest augmentation when no model provider is configured

## Symptom

`mvn test` hangs indefinitely during Quarkus augmentation when `quarkus-langchain4j-core` is a compile-scope dependency of a non-extension library module and no AI model provider is configured. No error message, no timeout — the build just blocks.

## Context

Occurs when a library module (Jandex-indexed, not a full Quarkus extension) adds `io.quarkiverse.langchain4j:quarkus-langchain4j-core` as a compile dependency to use the `EmbeddingModel` interface. During `@QuarkusTest` augmentation, the Quarkus extension activates and waits for a model provider that never arrives.

## What was tried

Adding `quarkus-langchain4j-core` as a `provided` scope — still stalls because the extension's deployment artifact is resolved transitively during augmentation.

## Root cause

`quarkus-langchain4j-core` is a Quarkus extension (has both runtime and deployment jars). When it appears on the augmentation classpath, Quarkus activates it and expects at least one model provider (OpenAI, Ollama, etc.) to be configured. Without one, augmentation stalls waiting for the CDI bean to resolve.

## Fix

Use `dev.langchain4j:langchain4j-core` (the plain Java library, **not** the Quarkus extension) as the compile dependency. This provides the `EmbeddingModel` interface for compilation without triggering Quarkus extension activation:

```xml
<dependency>
  <groupId>dev.langchain4j</groupId>
  <artifactId>langchain4j-core</artifactId>
  <version>1.13.0</version>
</dependency>
```

For CDI injection, use `Instance<EmbeddingModel>` instead of `@Inject EmbeddingModel`:

```java
@Inject
public MyBean(final Instance<EmbeddingModel> modelInstance) {
    this.model = modelInstance.isResolvable() ? modelInstance.get() : null;
}
```

When no provider is configured, `instance.isResolvable()` returns `false` gracefully. The consuming app brings `quarkus-langchain4j-openai` (or any other provider extension), which satisfies the CDI bean at their own augmentation time.

## Why this is non-obvious

The symptom is a hang with no error — not a compilation failure or a clear exception. The natural instinct is to add the Quarkus extension for its CDI integration, but the correct separation is: library module uses the plain Java API, consuming application brings the Quarkus extension.

### Why this fix

The plain Java `langchain4j-core` provides the same `EmbeddingModel` interface without triggering extension activation. `Instance<EmbeddingModel>` makes the dependency optional at CDI resolution time, so the library works both with and without a configured provider.

**See also:** GE-20260422-3242bf — Use Instance<T> for optional CDI injection — resolves gracefully when no bean exists

### Alternatives considered

- `provided` scope: still triggers augmentation because the extension's deployment artifact is resolved transitively
- Mocking `EmbeddingModel` via `@InjectMock` in every `@QuarkusTest`: works but requires all existing tests to be updated

entries.title: Vert.x Mutiny PgPool.getConnection() returns SqlConnection wrapper — casting to PgConnection throws ClassCastException
entries.body:
Vert.x Mutiny PgPool.getConnection() returns SqlConnection wrapper — casting to PgConnection throws ClassCastException

## Symptom

```
java.lang.ClassCastException: Cannot cast io.vertx.mutiny.sqlclient.SqlConnection
    to io.vertx.mutiny.pgclient.PgConnection
```

Occurs when trying to use `pool.getConnection()` to get a `PgConnection` for calling
`notificationHandler()` (needed for PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY).

## Context

Needed a dedicated `PgConnection` held open for LISTEN/NOTIFY (PostgreSQL pub/sub for
distributed SSE fan-out). `PgPool.getConnection()` is the natural way to get a connection
from an injected pool.

## What was tried

```java
// Fails at runtime with ClassCastException
pool.getConnection()
    .onItem().castTo(PgConnection.class) // ClassCastException
    .subscribe()...
```

Also tried: direct cast `(PgConnection) conn` — same result.

## Root cause

Vert.x Mutiny codegen generates separate wrapper classes for each Vert.x type. 
`io.vertx.mutiny.sqlclient.SqlConnection` and `io.vertx.mutiny.pgclient.PgConnection`
are separate classes even though their underlying delegates share the `PgConnection`
delegate hierarchy. The Mutiny wrappers do NOT mirror the delegate inheritance chain.

`PgPool.getConnection()` is inherited from `io.vertx.mutiny.sqlclient.Pool` and returns
`Uni<SqlConnection>` — not `Uni<PgConnection>`. The actual delegate IS a
`io.vertx.pgclient.PgConnection`, but the Mutiny wrapper is `SqlConnection`.

## Fix

```java
// PgPool always yields PgConnection delegates — unwrap and re-wrap.
pool.getConnection().subscribe().with(conn -> {
    final io.vertx.pgclient.PgConnection pgDelegate =
            (io.vertx.pgclient.PgConnection) conn.getDelegate();
    final PgConnection pgConn = PgConnection.newInstance(pgDelegate);
    pgConn.notificationHandler(n -> handleNotification(n.getPayload()));
    pgConn.query("LISTEN " + CHANNEL).execute().subscribe().with(...);
});
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The Mutiny class hierarchy docs don't mention that wrapper types are generated flat
(no inheritance mirroring). A developer would reasonably assume that since `PgPool`
extends `Pool` and yields `PgConnection` instances at the delegate level, the Mutiny
wrappers would behave similarly. They don't. `.getDelegate()` + `.newInstance()` is
the only way to cross wrapper type boundaries.

entries.title: Instance<T>.handles() returns Iterable<? extends Instance.Handle<T>> — incompatible with Iterable<InstanceHandle<T>>
entries.body:
Instance<T>.handles() returns Iterable<? extends Instance.Handle<T>> — incompatible with Iterable<InstanceHandle<T>>

## Symptom

Code injects `Instance<T>`, calls `.handles()`, then tries to assign or pass the result to `Iterable<InstanceHandle<T>>`:

```java
@Inject Instance<MessageObserver> observers;

// Somewhere:
Iterable<InstanceHandle<MessageObserver>> handles = observers.handles(); // COMPILE ERROR
```

Or a method parameter typed as `Iterable<InstanceHandle<T>>` is called with `observers.handles()`:

```java
void dispatch(Iterable<InstanceHandle<MessageObserver>> handles) { ... }
dispatch(observers.handles()); // COMPILE ERROR
```

Compiler error: `incompatible types: Iterable<capture#1 of ? extends Instance.Handle<T>> cannot be converted to Iterable<InstanceHandle<T>>`.

## Context

Occurs when refactoring code to use `Instance.handles()` for proper `@Dependent` lifecycle management. `InstanceHandle<T>` (Quarkus Arc) and `Instance.Handle<T>` (CDI standard) are related but distinct — `InstanceHandle<T>` extends `Instance.Handle<T>`, but Java's invariant generics mean `Iterable<InstanceHandle<T>>` is NOT assignable from `Iterable<? extends Instance.Handle<T>>`.

## Root Cause

`Instance.handles()` is declared to return `Iterable<? extends Instance.Handle<T>>` (CDI standard type with upper-bounded wildcard). Even though Quarkus Arc implements this with `InstanceHandle<T>` objects at runtime, the static type is the CDI wildcard form.

Because Java generics are invariant, `Iterable<InstanceHandle<T>>` (no wildcard) is not a supertype of `Iterable<? extends Instance.Handle<T>>`. The assignment fails at compile time.

## Fix

Use the upper-bounded wildcard in the receiving type/parameter:

```java
// Correct parameter type:
static void dispatch(Iterable<? extends Instance.Handle<MessageObserver>> handles) { ... }

// Or assign with wildcard:
Iterable<? extends Instance.Handle<MessageObserver>> handles = observers.handles();
```

For test anonymous classes implementing `InstanceHandle<T>`, the wildcard still accepts them because `InstanceHandle<T>` IS-A `? extends Instance.Handle<T>`.

Prefer `Instance.Handle<T>` (CDI standard) over `InstanceHandle<T>` (Arc-specific) in method signatures — it works across both the production CDI context and test anonymous implementations.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The developer sees `InstanceHandle<T>` objects in the debugger (Arc's concrete type), and `InstanceHandle<T>` extends `Instance.Handle<T>`, so the assignment "obviously should work". The wildcard is invisible — `handles()` appears to return a simple iterable. The Java generics variance rule (invariance by default) only surfaces at compile time with a cryptic type-inference error.

**See also:** [GE-20260530-680285](GE-20260530-680285.md) — CDI 4.1 Instance.handles() returns Iterable not Stream — use Arc's InjectableInstance.handlesStream()

entries.title: Span.wrap(SpanContext).makeCurrent() creates OTel trace context in tests without SDK
entries.body:
Span.wrap(SpanContext).makeCurrent() creates OTel trace context in tests without SDK

## Technique

To test OTel-aware code that reads `Span.current()`, use `Span.wrap(SpanContext)`
to create a span backed by a specific context, then make it current with
`span.makeCurrent()`. This works with the OTel API alone — no SDK, no tracer,
no `@QuarkusTest` OTel configuration needed.

```java
SpanContext ctx = SpanContext.create(
        "4bf92f3577b34da6a3ce929d0e0e4736",
        "00f067aa0ba902b7",
        TraceFlags.getSampled(),
        TraceState.getDefault());

try (Scope ignored = Span.wrap(ctx).makeCurrent()) {
    // Span.current() now returns a span with the above context
    // — test your OTel-aware code here
    Optional<String> traceId = provider.currentTraceId();
    assertThat(traceId).contains("4bf92f3577b34da6a3ce929d0e0e4736");
}
```

## Context

Any code that reads `Span.current().getSpanContext()` can be tested this way —
unit tests, `@QuarkusTest` integration tests, or anything in between. The scope
is thread-local and automatically cleaned up by the try-with-resources.

## Why this is non-obvious

The natural instinct is to configure a real OTel SDK (add `quarkus-opentelemetry`,
set up an exporter, create a `Tracer`). That works but adds significant test
infrastructure. `Span.wrap()` is an OTel API method specifically designed for
wrapping an existing `SpanContext` in a non-recording span — it exists, is stable,
and requires nothing beyond the API jar, which is often already transitively present.

**See also:** [GE-20260523-bd68ba](GE-20260523-bd68ba.md) — @ObservesAsync CDI handlers run without the caller's OTel span — traceId silently null

entries.title: Hardcoding a consumer-specific @PersistenceUnit in a generic Quarkus extension silently breaks all other consumers
OpenClaw /v1/chat/completions requires model='openclaw', not the upstream provider model ID
entries.body:
OpenClaw /v1/chat/completions requires model='openclaw', not the upstream provider model ID

## OpenClaw /v1/chat/completions requires model='openclaw', not the upstream provider model ID

**ID:** GE-20260614-328420
**Stack:** OpenClaw v2026.6.x

### Symptom

Calling OpenClaw's OpenAI-compatible chat completions endpoint with a provider model
name returns HTTP 400:

```json
{"error":{"message":"Invalid `model`. Use `openclaw` or `openclaw/<agentId>`.",
 "type":"invalid_request_error"}}
```

### Context

When `gateway.http.endpoints.chatCompletions.enabled: true` is set in `openclaw.json`,
OpenClaw exposes `POST /v1/chat/completions`. The natural assumption is to use the
configured upstream model ID (e.g. `"mock-llm/mock-model"`, `"anthropic/claude-opus-4-6"`).

### Root cause / Undocumented behaviour

The `model` field in the request does not name the upstream LLM. It names the OpenClaw
agent. OpenClaw IS the model from the caller's perspective — it receives the request,
routes it through its agent pipeline, and calls the configured upstream provider
internally. The upstream provider model is determined by the agent's config, not the
request.

Valid model values:
- `"openclaw"` — uses the default agent
- `"openclaw/<agentId>"` — routes to a specific named agent

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:18789/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"openclaw","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}'
```

OpenClaw then calls its configured upstream provider (e.g. `mock-llm/mock-model` via
WireMock, or `anthropic/claude-opus-4-6`) transparently.

### Why this is non-obvious

The OpenClaw docs describe the chatCompletions endpoint as OpenAI-compatible, which
implies `model` should identify the LLM. The error message does clarify the correct
values, but the architectural reason (OpenClaw = the model, upstream = internal) is
not explained anywhere in the docs. The distinction only becomes clear after reading
the error response.

entries.title: DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService is a non-obvious CDI dependency of SignalReceivedEventHandler — excluding it causes deployment failure
entries.body:
DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService is a non-obvious CDI dependency of SignalReceivedEventHandler — excluding it causes deployment failure

## Symptom

CDI deployment fails when `SignalReceivedEventHandler` is active but
`DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService` is excluded via `quarkus.arc.exclude-types`.
The error names `WorkerExecutionRecoveryService` as unsatisfied — not `SignalReceivedEventHandler`
— so the connection between the two is non-obvious.

## Context

When building a test profile that needs `SignalReceivedEventHandler` active (e.g. to test
case completion driven by `CaseHubRuntime.signal()`), you cannot simply remove the handler
from the exclude list. `SignalReceivedEventHandler` injects `WorkerExecutionRecoveryService`,
which is only satisfied by `DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService`. Both must be active together.

## Root Cause

`SignalReceivedEventHandler.onSignalReceived()` has a recovery path:

```java
return recoveryService
    .loadOrRestoreCaseInstance(event.caseId())
    .chain(instance -> applySignal(instance, event));
```

If the case instance is not in cache, it delegates to `WorkerExecutionRecoveryService`.
The injection point is eager — CDI validates it at augmentation time regardless of whether
the recovery path is actually hit.

## Fix

In your test profile's `quarkus.arc.exclude-types`, either:

1. **Include both handlers** — remove both `SignalReceivedEventHandler` AND
   `DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService` from the exclude list simultaneously.

2. **Exclude both** — if you don't need signal handling, exclude both.

Never activate one without the other.

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The class is called `SignalReceivedEventHandler` — the name gives no indication it needs
recovery infrastructure. `DefaultWorkerExecutionRecoveryService` is primarily associated
with the worker execution recovery path, not with signal handling.

**See also:** [GE-20260529-b5723e](GE-20260529-b5723e.md) — casehub-engine (full runtime) as compile dep causes 31+ CDI deployment failures

ProjectionResult.isEmpty() is cursor-based, not state-based — filtering projections produce isEmpty()==false even when no messages matched
entries.body:
ProjectionResult.isEmpty() is cursor-based, not state-based — filtering projections produce isEmpty()==false even when no messages matched

## Symptom

After projecting a channel through a filtering `ChannelProjection` decorator (e.g. one that skips messages with `round > maxRound`), calling `result.isEmpty()` returns `false` even though the resulting state is identical to `projection.identity()` — no messages contributed to the state.

Code that checks `result.isEmpty()` to branch to a "no content" sentinel fails silently, calling `renderer.render(identityState)` instead and producing output like `# Review Summary\n**Updated:** ...\n\n---\n\n` with no actual content.

## Context

`ProjectionResult.isEmpty()` is defined as:

```java
public boolean isEmpty() {
    return lastMessageId == null;
}
```

`lastMessageId` is the ID of the last message **scanned** by `ProjectionService.fold()`, not the last message that **contributed** to the state:

```java
for (final var msg : messages) {
    state = projection.apply(state, mapper.toMessageView(msg));
    lastId = msg.id;  // updated for EVERY message, even if apply() returned state unchanged
}
return new ProjectionResult<>(state, lastId);
```

When a decorator projection returns `state` unchanged for every message (because all messages have `round > maxRound`), the cursor still advances to the last scanned message ID. `isEmpty()` therefore returns `false` — the channel was not empty, it was fully scanned.

## Root Cause

`isEmpty()` answers "was the channel empty at scan time?" not "did any message contribute to the state?". A filtering decorator that skips all messages leaves the cursor at the last scanned message ID, making the result look non-empty even though the materialized state is `identity()`.

## Fix

Check state content explicitly after a filtering projection:

```java
// Wrong — isEmpty() checks the cursor, not whether messages matched
String summary = result.isEmpty()
    ? "No activity."
    : renderer.render(result.state());

// Right — check actual state content for domain-specific emptiness
ReviewState state = result.state();
String summary = (state.points().isEmpty()
        && state.memos().isEmpty()
        && state.subTaskFindings().isEmpty())
    ? "No activity up to round " + round + "."
    : renderer.render(state);
```

The emptiness check must be domain-specific — the caller knows which fields constitute "empty" for its state type.

## Why Non-Obvious

`isEmpty()` is the standard Java idiom for "nothing here." Developers reading `ProjectionResult.isEmpty()` reasonably expect it to mean "the projection produced no content." The actual semantics — "no messages were scanned" — are cursor-based and only detectable by reading the `ProjectionResult` record definition. The Javadoc documents the contract correctly (`lastMessageId == null` when the channel was empty at scan time) but the mismatch with developer expectations is easy to miss when writing decorator projections that filter rather than accumulate.

## Alternatives Considered

- Adding a `hasContent()` method to `ProjectionResult` that delegates to the state — rejected because it would require `ProjectionResult` to know about the state's structure (loses genericity)
- Using the incremental overload (`project(channelId, previous, projection)`) — not applicable here since we want a fresh full scan, just filtered

entries.title: QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() scopes a CDI @Alternative to a single @QuarkusTest class
entries.body:
QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() scopes a CDI @Alternative to a single @QuarkusTest class

## QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() scopes a CDI @Alternative to a single @QuarkusTest class

### Stack
Quarkus 3.32.2, JUnit 5

### Symptom
A test double (e.g. `FailingWorkItemStore`) needs to be the active CDI bean for ONE
test class but must not interfere with other `@QuarkusTest` classes in the same module.
Adding it to global `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in `application.properties` leaks
it into all test classes — even those that don't need it.

### Context
`@QuarkusTest` classes share a single Quarkus application context (restarted only when
the `@TestProfile` changes). Test doubles listed in global `selected-alternatives` become
active for every class, creating hidden coupling and pollution risk.

### Fix

1. Define an inner `Profile` class implementing `QuarkusTestProfile`:

```java
@QuarkusTest
@TestProfile(MyTest.Profile.class)
class MyTest {

public static class Profile implements QuarkusTestProfile {
        @Override
        public Set<Class<?>> getEnabledAlternatives() {
            return Set.of(MyTestDouble.class);
        }
    }

@Alternative @Priority(2) @ApplicationScoped
    public static class MyTestDouble implements TheInterface {
        // test-only implementation
    }

// tests...
}
```

2. Remove the test double from global `selected-alternatives` in `application.properties`.

`getEnabledAlternatives()` activates the listed classes *in addition to* global
`selected-alternatives` — only for this profile. Other test classes run without it.
Quarkus restarts the application once when switching to/from this profile.

### Why this is non-obvious
Most Quarkus CDI testing examples use `quarkus.arc.selected-alternatives` in
`application.properties`, which is global and bleeds across all test classes. The
`QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives()` API exists specifically for per-class
scoping but is not prominently documented alongside CDI testing patterns. The key insight:
`@TestProfile` triggers a Quarkus restart, making it the cleanest isolation boundary
available short of a separate module.

### Why this fix
Global `selected-alternatives` is the obvious approach but couples all test classes to
the test double. `getEnabledAlternatives()` activates the alternative only when the
profile is active — zero leakage. The one-restart cost per profile boundary is acceptable
in test suites.

**See also:** GE-20260415-884e48 — @Alternative @Priority globally activates in CDI 4.0

**See also:** [GE-20260520-e15ff0](GE-20260520-e15ff0.md) — QuarkusTestProfile.getConfigOverrides() for per-class config isolation | [GE-20260521-4de4f1](GE-20260521-4de4f1.md) — getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely (contradicts constraints above — verify against your version)

**See also:** [GE-20260601-cee623](GE-20260601-cee623.md) — QuarkusTestProfile.getEnabledAlternatives() replaces selected-alternatives entirely — contradicts constraints on this entry; verify against your Quarkus version

entries.title: quarkus.datasource.reactive=false suppresses Hibernate Reactive boot when extension is on classpath but no reactive pool exists
entries.body:
quarkus.datasource.reactive=false suppresses Hibernate Reactive boot when extension is on classpath but no reactive pool exists

## quarkus.datasource.reactive=false suppresses Hibernate Reactive boot when extension is on classpath but no reactive pool exists

**ID:** GE-20260420-45d53b
**Stack:** Quarkus 3.32.2

**What it does:** Setting `quarkus.datasource.reactive=false` in `application.properties` prevents Hibernate Reactive from attempting to acquire a reactive (Vert.x) connection pool at startup, even when `quarkus-hibernate-reactive-panache` is on the classpath and has registered reactive persistence units at build time.

**Where it works:** Test `application.properties`, per-profile properties, and main `application.properties`.

```properties
# Prevents FastBootHibernateReactivePersistenceProvider from failing
# when no reactive datasource is configured
quarkus.datasource.reactive=false
```

### Why non-obvious
Nothing in the Quarkus extension developer docs mentions this property as a reactive-boot suppressor. The reactive extension activates at build time based on classpath scanning; suppressing it at runtime via a datasource flag is counter-intuitive. The natural search path (Hibernate Reactive docs, `@Alternative`, `quarkus.hibernate-reactive.*` properties) doesn't lead here. Found by trial and error.

**See also:** GE-20260420-33b95f (specific H2+test scenario), GE-20260420-daf5dc (optional dep activation gotcha), GE-20260420-dcec35 (extension forces HR boot even with @Alternative)

entries.title: @LookupIfProperty for conditional CDI bean activation via Instance<> — cleaner than inert-bean pattern
entries.body:
@LookupIfProperty for conditional CDI bean activation via Instance<> — cleaner than inert-bean pattern

### Context
A Quarkus application ships multiple provider implementations of an SPI (e.g. `DeviceProvider`) in one Docker image. Each provider should be independently enableable via config (`provider.enabled=true/false`). Disabled providers must be invisible to `Instance<DeviceProvider>` iteration — not just "present but returning empty results."

### What a skilled developer would normally do
Guard in `@PostConstruct`: check `enabled`, skip initialization if false. Make every method return a no-op result (`discover()` → empty list, `dispatch()` → FAILED, `status()` → DISCONNECTED). This is the "inert-bean" pattern — the bean exists but does nothing.

Problems with inert beans:
- The bean IS discovered by `Instance<DeviceProvider>` — callers see a broken provider
- `@PostConstruct` still runs (and may fail if config properties are missing)
- Every method needs guard logic
- SmallRye Config still validates all `@ConfigMapping` properties at startup (see GE-20260626-e0ecaf)

### Technique
Use `@LookupIfProperty` from Quarkus ARC:

```java
@ApplicationScoped
@LookupIfProperty(name = "casehub.iot.homeassistant.enabled", stringValue = "true")
public class HomeAssistantProvider implements DeviceProvider { ... }
```

When `enabled` is absent or `false`:
- The bean is never instantiated
- `@PostConstruct` never runs
- `Instance<DeviceProvider>` iteration skips it entirely
- No guard logic needed in any method

Combined with `Optional<String>` on all `@ConfigMapping` properties (GE-20260626-e0ecaf), this allows disabled providers to exist on the classpath without causing config validation failures or appearing in the provider registry.

### Why this is non-obvious
`@LookupIfProperty` is less well-known than `@IfBuildProperty` (which is build-time only and prevents runtime reconfiguration). The naming — "lookup if" vs "if build" — doesn't clearly convey the difference. Many developers reach for `@IfBuildProperty` first, discover it prevents runtime toggling in Docker images, and then fall back to the inert-bean pattern instead of finding `@LookupIfProperty`.

entries.title: @Alternative on a CDI bean silently suppresses all @DefaultBean beans of the same type — they vanish from the container entirely
Library jar with @ApplicationScoped beans silently overrides @DefaultBean alternatives in consuming app
entries.body:
Library jar with @ApplicationScoped beans silently overrides @DefaultBean alternatives in consuming app

### Symptom
Adding `casehub-openclaw-casehub` as a Maven dependency to `casehub-life` causes integration tests to fail with:
```
IllegalArgumentException: Unknown channel purpose 'coordination' for case <uuid>
```
No code changes — just the dependency addition.

### Context
`casehub-openclaw-casehub` contains `OpenClawCaseChannelProvider @ApplicationScoped` which implements `CaseChannelProvider`. The engine provides `NoOpCaseChannelProvider @DefaultBean`. When the openclaw jar lands on the classpath, Quarkus CDI discovers the `@ApplicationScoped` bean and activates it — overriding the `@DefaultBean` alternative that life was using.

`OpenClawCaseChannelProvider` validates channel purposes against `NormativeChannelLayout` (work/observe/oversight only). The engine passes "coordination" as a purpose — accepted by the NoOp, rejected by the OpenClaw provider.

Additionally, `OpenClawWorkerStatusListener`, `OpenClawAgentRegistry`, `OversightGateService`, and other heartbeat-mode beans activate unnecessarily. These are designed for OpenClaw's provisioner/heartbeat integration mode — not needed for the direct-call bridge.

### What was tried (didn't work)
1. Assuming only `DirectCallBridge` and `DirectCallDeliveryResource` would activate — wrong; all `@ApplicationScoped` beans in the jar activate
2. Excluding only the obviously conflicting `OpenClawCaseChannelProvider` — insufficient; other beans (WorkerStatusListener, AgentRegistry) also caused interference

### Root cause
Quarkus CDI discovers ALL beans in all JARs on the classpath. An `@ApplicationScoped` bean always takes precedence over a `@DefaultBean` for the same type. There is no mechanism to selectively activate beans from a library JAR — it's all or nothing.

### Fix
Exclude all heartbeat/provisioner-mode beans from `casehub-openclaw-casehub` that life doesn't need. Keep only `DirectCallBridge` and `DirectCallDeliveryResource`:

```properties
quarkus.arc.exclude-types=\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.OpenClawCaseChannelProvider,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.ReactiveOpenClawCaseChannelProvider,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.OpenClawWorkerProvisioner,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.ReactiveOpenClawWorkerProvisioner,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.OpenClawChannelBackend,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.ChannelContextWindowObserver,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.OpenClawAgentRegistry,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.OpenClawCasehubConfig,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.OpenClawWorkerStatusListener,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.OversightGateDispatcher,\
  io.casehub.openclaw.casehub.OversightGateService
```

Must be applied to BOTH `application.properties` and `test/resources/application.properties`.

### Why this is non-obvious
The dependency was added for `DirectCallBridge` and `DirectCallDeliveryResource` only. The expectation was that unused beans would remain dormant. In fact, CDI activates every discovered bean — and `@ApplicationScoped` beats `@DefaultBean` unconditionally. The error message ("Unknown channel purpose") gives no indication that the root cause is a CDI bean activation from a transitive dependency.

entries.title: Quarkus CDI does not scan @ApplicationScoped beans in plain JAR module dependencies
entries.body:
Quarkus CDI does not scan @ApplicationScoped beans in plain JAR module dependencies

## Behaviour

In a Quarkus multi-module Maven project, if a plain library module (no Quarkus Maven plugin, `packaging=jar`) contains `@ApplicationScoped` CDI beans and is added as a `<dependency>` of the Quarkus application module, those beans are NOT discovered by CDI. Injection of these beans at runtime fails with "Unsatisfied dependency for type X".

## Context

Occurs when splitting a single-module Quarkus app into a library module + Quarkus app module. The library beans compile fine and the JAR is on the classpath, but Quarkus CDI ignores them.

## Root Cause

Quarkus CDI bean discovery only indexes classes in JARs that are either: (a) the application itself, (b) have a Jandex index (`META-INF/jandex.idx`), or (c) are explicitly listed in `quarkus.index-dependency`. Plain JARs without Jandex are not scanned.

## Fix

Add to `claudony-app/src/main/resources/application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.group-id=com.example
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.artifact-id=mylib-core
```

Or add the Jandex Maven plugin to the library module's `pom.xml`:

```xml
<plugin>
    <groupId>io.smallrye</groupId>
    <artifactId>jandex-maven-plugin</artifactId>
    <version>3.1.2</version>
    <executions>
        <execution>
            <goals><goal>jandex</goal></goals>
        </execution>
    </executions>
</plugin>
```

## Why Undocumented

The Quarkus docs for multi-module projects mention the Jandex plugin as an option but do not clearly state that without it (or `index-dependency`), CDI beans in library JARs are silently ignored. The error message "Unsatisfied dependency" doesn't hint at the indexing problem — it looks like the bean simply doesn't exist.

**See also:** [GE-20260421-03fa72](GE-20260421-03fa72.md) — Jandex Maven plugin approach to same problem; [GE-20260423-a01832](GE-20260423-a01832.md) — @Alternative test JARs same root cause; [GE-0133](GE-0133.md) — CDI silently ignores @ApplicationScoped beans in third-party JARs without Jandex
**See also:** [GE-20260427-c2b84f](GE-20260427-c2b84f.md) — same CDI scanning root cause for @Alternative beans in test-helper JARs

**See also:** GE-20260428-9311f8 — indexed beans cause CDI collision (opposite problem)

entries.title: @ApplicationScoped CDI beans are always-active in Quarkus — safe to call from any thread
Two @Startup @ApplicationScoped Quarkus beans have no guaranteed initialization order — @PostConstruct side effects are unreliable across beans
@ApplicationScoped bean present in JAR bytecode is invisible to Quarkus ARC without a Jandex index
entries.body:
@ApplicationScoped bean present in JAR bytecode is invisible to Quarkus ARC without a Jandex index

## Symptom

ARC reports `"Unsatisfied dependency for type com.example.SomeBean"` at augmentation time. `javap -p -verbose` on the `.class` file inside the JAR confirms `@ApplicationScoped` is present as a `RuntimeVisibleAnnotation`. The class is on the classpath.

## Context

A third-party or internal library JAR has `@ApplicationScoped` (or other CDI scope) beans but was not built with the `jandex-maven-plugin` and ships no `META-INF/jandex.idx`. Quarkus ARC relies on Jandex for efficient bean discovery — without an index it does not scan the JAR by default.

## Root cause

Quarkus ARC uses Jandex as its primary bean discovery mechanism. JARs without a Jandex index and without `beans.xml` are treated as non-bean-archives and skipped entirely. The CDI annotations in bytecode are present but ARC never reads them.

## Fix

Add an explicit index-dependency entry in `application.properties`:

```properties
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.group-id=com.example
quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.artifact-id=mylib
```

Quarkus will then build an in-memory Jandex index for that JAR at augmentation time, discovering all CDI beans.

If the JAR should only be indexed in production (not in `@QuarkusTest`), use the `%prod.` profile prefix:

```properties
%prod.quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.group-id=com.example
%prod.quarkus.index-dependency.mylib.artifact-id=mylib
```

And add a separate test-scope entry to `src/test/resources/application.properties` if needed there.

## Verifying the JAR

```bash
python3 -c "
import zipfile
jar = 'path/to/mylib.jar'
with zipfile.ZipFile(jar) as z:
    has_jandex = any('jandex' in n for n in z.namelist())
    has_beans = any('beans.xml' in n for n in z.namelist())
    print(f'jandex={has_jandex}, beans.xml={has_beans}')
"
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The error "Unsatisfied dependency" implies the class doesn't exist or isn't a CDI bean — it says nothing about indexing. `javap` shows the annotation is genuinely in the bytecode. There is no Quarkus warning at augmentation time that a JAR was skipped due to missing Jandex.

**See also:** [GE-20260522-adb5cd](GE-20260522-adb5cd.md) — moving bean from app module to library JAR breaks CDI discovery | [GE-20260427-c2b84f](../quarkus/GE-20260427-c2b84f.md) — @Alternative beans in test-helper jar invisible without Jandex

**See also:** [GE-20260601-7a3b38](GE-20260601-7a3b38.md) — @DefaultBean implementations invisible when supertype JAR has no Jandex index

**See also:** [GE-20260609-18a0b1](GE-20260609-18a0b1.md) — quarkus.index-dependency for a JAR that ships META-INF/jandex.idx double-scans it (opposite problem: don't add if already indexed)

**See also:** [GE-20260615-c234fc](GE-20260615-c234fc.md) — @DefaultBean in module without quarkus-arc is silently ignored

entries.title: Moving @ApplicationScoped bean from Quarkus app module to library JAR silently breaks CDI discovery
entries.body:
Moving @ApplicationScoped bean from Quarkus app module to library JAR silently breaks CDI discovery

### Symptom
`@QuarkusTest` startup fails with N "Unsatisfied dependency for type X" errors.
No compile error. The bean is fully visible to the Java compiler.

### Context
A CDI `@ApplicationScoped` bean is extracted from the Quarkus application module
into a shared library JAR (a Maven dependency). Works fine before the move.

### Root Cause
Quarkus only auto-indexes the application module for CDI bean discovery. Library
JARs on the classpath are **not** scanned unless explicitly listed in
`quarkus.index-dependency`. Java compilation sees the class; the Quarkus CDI
container does not.

### Fix
Add to every `src/test/resources/application.properties` that needs the bean:
```properties
quarkus.index-dependency.<key>.group-id=<groupId>
quarkus.index-dependency.<key>.artifact-id=<artifactId>
```
Required in every module that runs `@QuarkusTest` and injects the bean — not
just the module that moved it.

### Why This Is Non-Obvious
The Java compiler resolves the type from the dependency JAR — it compiles and
links correctly. Nothing in the build output signals the problem. The failure
only appears at `@QuarkusTest` application startup, and the error message
("Unsatisfied dependency") looks like the bean doesn't exist, not that it
isn't indexed.

**See also:** [GE-20260523-afab1d](GE-20260523-afab1d.md) — @ApplicationScoped bean in JAR bytecode invisible to Quarkus ARC without Jandex index | [GE-20260427-c2b84f](../quarkus/GE-20260427-c2b84f.md) — @Alternative beans in test-helper jar invisible without Jandex

**See also:** [GE-20260610-204ddb](GE-20260610-204ddb.md) — stale core module jar breaks CDI resolution

**See also:** [GE-20260603-268164](GE-20260603-268164.md) — quarkus:build on a library JAR forces CDI validation on compile-scope only

entries.title: Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422
entries.body:
Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY doesn't exist in Jakarta EE 9 JAX-RS — use raw integer 422

## Symptom

Code compiles with a `Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` reference in a `WebApplicationException` or `Response.status()` call, but the IDE shows an error and the build fails:

```
error: cannot find symbol
  symbol:   variable UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
  location: class Response.Status
```

## Root cause

`Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY` (HTTP 422) was added in JAX-RS 3.1 / Jakarta EE 10. Projects targeting Jakarta EE 9 / JAX-RS 3.0 (which Quarkus 3.x may use depending on the BOM) don't have this constant.

## Fix

Use the raw integer `422` directly:

```java
// Wrong (JAX-RS 3.0 / Jakarta EE 9):
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", Response.Status.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY);

// Correct:
throw new WebApplicationException("Not valid", 422);
```

Or create a constant:
```java
private static final int HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY = 422;
```

## Why this is non-obvious

The HTTP 422 status is well-established (WebDAV, RFC 4918) and commonly used for validation errors. Developers reasonably expect it to be in the `Response.Status` enum. The version split between EE 9 and EE 10 isn't surfaced by IDE autocomplete which may show UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY from a different JAR on the classpath.

Merging a branch with `--delete-branch` auto-closes any PR targeting that branch
entries.body:
Merging a branch with `--delete-branch` auto-closes any PR targeting that branch

## Merging a branch with `--delete-branch` auto-closes any PR targeting that branch

**ID:** GE-0175
**Stack:** GitHub (all versions)
**Symptom:** PR #B (targeting branch A) is silently closed — not retargeted — when branch A is merged and deleted. No warning, no retargeting prompt.
**Context:** Stacked PRs (B → A → main). Merging A with `--delete-branch` immediately closes B because its base branch no longer exists.

**Root cause:** GitHub closes PRs whose base branch is deleted rather than retargeting them — it cannot safely determine the new base. The PR must be manually recreated.

**Fix:**

Option 1 — Retarget before deleting:
```bash
gh pr merge A --merge          # no --delete-branch
gh pr edit B --base main
git push origin --delete A
```

Option 2 — Recreate after the fact:
```bash
gh pr create --base main --head B --title "..." --body "..."
```

**Prevention:** With stacked PRs, always retarget dependent PRs before deleting the base branch.

*Score: 11/15 · Non-obvious GitHub behaviour; error is silent with no warning · Reservation: GitHub-specific, may change if GitHub adds retargeting logic*

entries.title: CommitmentState.DECLINED not CANCELLED for DECLINE speech-act commitment outcome
entries.body:
CommitmentState.DECLINED not CANCELLED for DECLINE speech-act commitment outcome

## CommitmentState.DECLINED not CANCELLED for DECLINE speech-act commitment outcome

### Symptom

Compile error when asserting commitment state after a DECLINE message:

```
error: cannot find symbol
    assertThat(c.state).isEqualTo(CommitmentState.CANCELLED);
                                                  ^
  symbol:   variable CANCELLED
```

### Context

Any `@QuarkusTest` asserting the final commitment state after a `ReviewerChannelBackend` (or any Qhorus channel backend) dispatches a `DECLINE` message:

```java
assertThat(commitmentStore.findByCorrelationId(correlationId))
    .hasValueSatisfying(c -> assertThat(c.state).isEqualTo(CommitmentState.CANCELLED));  // ❌ WRONG
```

### Root cause

The `CommitmentState` enum does not have a `CANCELLED` variant. The full set of values is:

```java
OPEN, ACKNOWLEDGED, FULFILLED, DECLINED, FAILED, DELEGATED, EXPIRED
```

When a `MessageType.DECLINE` message is dispatched for a given `correlationId`, Qhorus resolves the outstanding commitment to `CommitmentState.DECLINED` — matching the speech-act message type exactly. The natural English word for this transition is "cancelled" (the obligation is cancelled by the decliner), but the enum models the action from the speech-act perspective: the responding party *declined*, not *cancelled*.

### Fix

Use `CommitmentState.DECLINED`:

```java
assertThat(commitmentStore.findByCorrelationId(correlationId))
    .hasValueSatisfying(c -> assertThat(c.state).isEqualTo(CommitmentState.DECLINED));  // ✅
```

The mapping from message type to commitment terminal state:
- `RESPONSE` → `FULFILLED`
- `DECLINE` → `DECLINED`
- Other terminal states (`FAILED`, `EXPIRED`, `DELEGATED`) are not produced by normal message dispatch

### Why this is non-obvious

An obligation is intuitively "cancelled" when the obligor refuses to fulfil it. The developer writes `CANCELLED` or `REJECTED` (both seem natural), but Qhorus uses `DECLINED` to mirror the corresponding speech-act message type (`MessageType.DECLINE`). The naming alignment makes sense retrospectively but produces a compile error with a confusing message on first encounter.

**See also:** [GE-20260521-e39ad1](GE-20260521-e39ad1.md) — CommitmentStore.findOpenByObligor() finds nothing for COMMAND messages | [GE-20260605-494ed0](GE-20260605-494ed0.md) — ChannelGateway.fanOut() delivers on virtual threads

**See also:** [GE-20260530-1a7e84](GE-20260530-1a7e84.md) — casehub-qhorus MessageDispatch.builder() requires inReplyTo + correlationId for response-type messages

entries.title: Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()
entries.body:
Mutiny Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor() does not exist — use getDefaultExecutor()

## What Happened

Code used `Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` to offload blocking work onto a worker pool in a Mutiny pipeline. The method name is logical (Mutiny has `runSubscriptionOn` for blocking work) but the method does not exist. The compile error is clear but the fix is non-obvious because no similar `getDefaultBlockingExecutor` pattern appears in Mutiny documentation.

## Why This Is Non-obvious

The Mutiny `Infrastructure` class has `getDefaultExecutor()` — the default worker pool suitable for blocking work when combined with `runSubscriptionOn`. There is no separately named "blocking" executor. The distinction between the IO thread and the worker pool is handled by `runSubscriptionOn` itself, not by a separate executor variant. The name "blocking" is a common mental model but doesn't map to any Mutiny API.

## Fix

```java
// Wrong — does not exist:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultBlockingExecutor())

// Correct:
.runSubscriptionOn(Infrastructure.getDefaultExecutor())
```

## Invalidation Triggers

Revisit if Mutiny adds a named `getDefaultBlockingExecutor()` alias in a future release.

**See also:** [GE-20260602-c38360](GE-20260602-c38360.md) — PanacheQuery.stream() does not exist in Quarkus Hibernate Reactive Panache

**See also:** [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist

**See also:** [GE-20260621-cb4492](GE-20260621-cb4492.md) — drools-engine artifact does not exist at Drools 10.1.0 | [GE-20260609-630b35](GE-20260609-630b35.md) — Uni.createFrom().runnable() does not exist (same class of API-that-doesn't-exist gotcha)

entries.title: TIMESTAMPTZ not recognised by H2 in MODE=PostgreSQL — use TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE
entries.body:
TIMESTAMPTZ not recognised by H2 in MODE=PostgreSQL — use TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE

## Context

Writing Flyway migrations for a Quarkus module that uses PostgreSQL in production and H2
with `MODE=PostgreSQL` in `@QuarkusTest`. The migration uses `TIMESTAMPTZ` (PostgreSQL's
abbreviated type name for `TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE`).

## Symptom

Flyway migration fails during `@QuarkusTest` startup with a SQL syntax error even though
`MODE=PostgreSQL` is configured. Production (PostgreSQL) works fine.

## Root Cause

`TIMESTAMPTZ` is a PostgreSQL-specific abbreviation. H2 implements only a subset of
PostgreSQL syntax in `MODE=PostgreSQL` — it recognises the full form
`TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE` but not the abbreviated `TIMESTAMPTZ`.

## Fix

Use the full form in Flyway migrations:

```sql
-- Fails in H2 even with MODE=PostgreSQL
created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,

-- Works in both PostgreSQL and H2 MODE=PostgreSQL
created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE NOT NULL,
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

PostgreSQL documentation and tooling widely use `TIMESTAMPTZ` — it appears in pg_dump
output, official PostgreSQL tutorials, and StackOverflow answers. `MODE=PostgreSQL` in H2
creates an expectation that PostgreSQL-idiomatic SQL will work. The error message points
to a SQL syntax failure, not to a type-name unsupported issue, making diagnosis indirect.

**See also:** [GE-20260603-86f2a9](GE-20260603-86f2a9.md) — H2 does not support INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING | [GE-20260605-0ffc19](GE-20260605-0ffc19.md) — H2 does not support SIMILAR TO in CHECK constraints

**See also:** [GE-20260617-03c1a3](GE-20260617-03c1a3.md) — H2 MODE=PostgreSQL ON CONFLICT syntax limitations

entries.title: static new ObjectMapper() silently fails to serialize Instant in Quarkus — use @Inject ObjectMapper
entries.body:
static new ObjectMapper() silently fails to serialize Instant in Quarkus — use @Inject ObjectMapper

## Symptom

An SSE endpoint (or any endpoint) that serialises `SessionResponse` objects silently returns `[]` (empty array) instead of the actual workers. No exception is visible to the caller — the exception is caught internally and the fallback value is returned. The server logs may show a `com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidDefinitionException` at DEBUG level.

## Context

`SessionResource` in Claudony had:
```java
private static final ObjectMapper MAPPER = new ObjectMapper();
```

`SessionResponse` contains `Instant createdAt` and `Instant lastActive`. Serializing a `List<SessionResponse>` through this `MAPPER` threw:
```
com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.exc.InvalidDefinitionException:
Java 8 date/time type `java.time.Instant` not supported by default:
add Module "com.fasterxml.jackson.datatype:jackson-datatype-jsr310"
```

The exception was caught in the `buildCaseSnapshot` helper and the fallback `"data: []"` was returned silently.

## Root Cause

`new ObjectMapper()` creates a bare instance with no modules registered. Quarkus configures its CDI-managed `ObjectMapper` bean with `JavaTimeModule` (and other modules like `Jdk8Module`) automatically. A static field bypasses this configuration.

## Fix

Replace the static field with CDI injection:

```java
// WRONG — no JavaTimeModule
private static final ObjectMapper MAPPER = new ObjectMapper();

// CORRECT — uses Quarkus-configured instance with all modules
@Inject
ObjectMapper MAPPER;
```

## Why This Is Non-Obvious

The class had a `static final` `ObjectMapper` that worked fine for other uses (simple POJOs without time fields). The failure only surfaced when the snapshot function started serialising `SessionResponse` with `Instant` fields. Because the exception was caught with a fallback, the endpoint returned valid JSON (`[]`) — not an error — making it very hard to notice.

casehub-work IllegalStateExceptionMapper silently maps IllegalStateException to HTTP 409
entries.body:
casehub-work IllegalStateExceptionMapper silently maps IllegalStateException to HTTP 409

### Symptom

REST endpoint returns HTTP 409 Conflict instead of the expected 500 Internal Server Error.
Happens when application code throws `IllegalStateException` for an infrastructure failure
(poll timeout, misconfiguration, unresolvable dependency).

### Context

Any application that depends on `casehub-work` and throws `IllegalStateException` from a
REST-invoked code path.

### Root cause

`casehub-work` JAR ships `io.casehub.work.runtime.api.IllegalStateExceptionMapper` — a
`jakarta.ws.rs.ext.ExceptionMapper<IllegalStateException>` that maps every
`IllegalStateException` to HTTP 409 Conflict. The mapper is registered globally via CDI
and affects any `IllegalStateException` thrown during a REST request in the application,
not just ones thrown from casehub-work code itself.

### Fix

Use a different exception type for infrastructure failures that should produce 500:

```java
// ❌ Produces HTTP 409 — mapped by casehub-work's IllegalStateExceptionMapper
throw new IllegalStateException("Agent did not reply for correlationId=" + id);

// ✅ Produces HTTP 500 — no mapper for generic RuntimeException
throw new RuntimeException("Agent did not reply for correlationId=" + id);
```

Alternatively, register a `@Priority(1)` exception mapper that overrides casehub-work's
for specific exception subtypes, or use a custom exception class that extends
`RuntimeException` directly.

### Why this is non-obvious

The mapper is shipped inside a transitive JAR (`casehub-work-0.2-SNAPSHOT.jar`) and is
nowhere documented. The symptom (409 instead of 500) gives no hint that an exception
mapper is involved — 409 Conflict is semantically about resource state, so developers
naturally investigate their domain logic rather than exception infrastructure. The 5-second
delay before the 409 (from a poll timeout) makes the connection even harder to spot.

entries.title: JAX-RS @Provider exception mapper bypassed when exception extends a type caught by a generic resource handler
WireMock: omitting the happy-path stub makes the wrong exception type the pre-flight test signal
entries.body:
WireMock: omitting the happy-path stub makes the wrong exception type the pre-flight test signal

## Context

Testing that a security pre-flight guard (e.g. `MemoryPermissions.assertTenant()`) fires
before any HTTP call in a REST-backed adapter. The implementation should throw
`SecurityException` before it reaches the wire.

The naive approach is to stub the happy path for item 0 and then call
`wireMock().verify(0, postRequestedFor(...))` after the exception. This works, but
it requires two assertions to tell the full story — and if both pass the test can still
hide subtle ordering bugs.

## Technique

Deliberately omit any stub for the endpoint that the pre-flight guard should prevent.

```java
@Test
void storeAll_any_tenant_mismatch_fires_zero_http_calls() {
    // No stub registered for POST /memories — intentional.
    // If implementation fires HTTP before the security check, WireMock returns
    // a no-match response (404), which becomes Mem0StoreException — NOT SecurityException.
    // The wrong exception type is the test failure signal.
    assertThrows(SecurityException.class, () ->
        store.storeAll(List.of(
            new MemoryInput("entity-1", DOMAIN, TENANT,       null, "ok",  Map.of()),
            new MemoryInput("entity-2", DOMAIN, OTHER_TENANT, null, "bad", Map.of())
        )));
    wireMock().verify(0, postRequestedFor(urlEqualTo("/memories")));
}
```

When WireMock has no stub for a request:
- The REST client receives a 404 (or a WireMock no-mapping response)
- The client wraps this in a `WebApplicationException`
- The adapter converts it to the domain exception (e.g. `Mem0StoreException`)
- `assertThrows(SecurityException.class, ...)` fails because `Mem0StoreException` was thrown

The `verify(0, ...)` line is redundant for correctness — the wrong exception type already
proves the HTTP call happened — but it makes the failure reason explicit.

## Why this is non-obvious

Developers instinctively stub the happy path to "protect" the test from failing for the
wrong reason. The impulse is: "item 0 might get through before item 1 throws, so I need
to stub it." But that instinct inverts the test logic for a pre-flight guard.

If the pre-flight fires correctly, item 0 never reaches the wire and no stub is needed.
If the pre-flight is missing, the un-stubbed call fails with the wrong exception —
which is exactly the signal you want.

Omitting the stub turns the test into a two-for-one check:
1. The exception type proves the pre-flight guard ran (not a transport error)
2. The `verify(0)` makes the HTTP count explicit for the reader

A test with the stub can still pass even if the pre-flight fires too late (after item 0
is sent) — the stub absorbs the call and the exception type appears correct by coincidence.

## Applicability

Works for any REST-backed adapter where:
- A security or validation guard should prevent HTTP calls
- The guard throws a typed exception distinguishable from transport errors
- WireMock is the HTTP mock

The pattern generalises: if the "should not happen" path produces a different
exception type than the "guard fired correctly" path, the absence of a stub
is the cheapest and strongest pre-flight test you can write.

**See also:** [GE-20260526-a08a81](GE-20260526-a08a81.md) — Quarkus MicroProfile REST Client throws WebApplicationException on non-2xx | [GE-20260526-286ac7](GE-20260526-286ac7.md) — use QuarkusTestResourceLifecycleManager to inject WireMock dynamic port

quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false disables @RolesAllowed enforcement in dev without a real OIDC server
entries.body:
quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false disables @RolesAllowed enforcement in dev without a real OIDC server

# quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false disables @RolesAllowed enforcement in dev — allows OIDC service to run without a real OIDC server

### Context
Running a Quarkus service with `quarkus-oidc` and `@RolesAllowed` annotations in dev mode
without a real OIDC server (no DevServices, no Keycloak). All guarded endpoints return 401
for every request.

### Technique
Set in `application.properties`:
```properties
%dev.quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false
%dev.quarkus.oidc.enabled=false
%dev.quarkus.keycloak.devservices.enabled=false
```

`quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode=false` activates `DevModeDisabledAuthorizationController`
(`@Alternative @Priority @Singleton` in `quarkus-security-runtime-spi`), which returns `false`
from `isAuthorizationEnabled()`. Both `EagerSecurityHandler` (REST path — checks at line 53
before processing the request) and `StandardSecurityCheckInterceptor` (CDI path — checks via
`controller.isAuthorizationEnabled()`) bail out early. `@RolesAllowed` is not enforced.

`quarkus.oidc.enabled=false` is also required — without it, OIDC intercepts requests before
the authorization check fires, returning 401 for missing tokens even with auth disabled.

### Why this is non-obvious
The natural dev-mode approaches are: (a) spin up Keycloak DevServices (60–90s cold start,
heavyweight), or (b) configure a real OIDC server. `quarkus.security.auth.enabled-in-dev-mode`
is not mentioned in the main OIDC documentation; it appears only in `quarkus-security-runtime-spi`
source as the activation mechanism for `DevModeDisabledAuthorizationController`. The interceptor
mechanism is two-level — disabling OIDC alone is insufficient because the authorization
interceptor fires independently of the authentication mechanism.

### Why this fix
Over disabling DevServices alone: `quarkus.keycloak.devservices.enabled=false` without the
auth flag still returns 401 — the security framework enforces `@RolesAllowed` against anonymous
identity regardless of whether an auth mechanism is registered. The auth flag is the only clean
way to bypass the enforcement layer itself.
